results.original_text:
【釋名】凍青。

藏器曰︰冬月青翠，故名冬青。江東人呼為凍青。

【集解】藏器曰︰冬青木肌白，有紋作象齒笏。其葉堪染緋。李邕云︰冬青出五台山，葉似椿，子赤如郁李，微酸性熱。與此小異，當是兩種冬青。

時珍曰︰凍青亦女貞別種也，山中時有之。但以葉微團而子赤者為凍青，葉長而子黑者為女貞。按︰《救荒本草》云︰凍青樹高丈許，樹似枸骨子樹而極茂盛。又葉似楂子樹葉而小，亦似椿葉微窄而頭頗圓，不尖。

五月開細白花，結子如豆大，紅色。其嫩芽炸熟，水浸去苦味，淘洗，五味調之可食。

子及木皮

【氣味】甘、苦，涼，無毒。

【主治】浸酒，去風虛，補益肌膚。皮之功同（藏器）。
燒灰，入面膏，治皸瘃，滅瘢痕，殊效（蘇頌）。
results.translation:
**[Explanation of Names]** Also written Dòngqīng [Frozen Green].

Chen Cangqi states: It remains green and lush through the winter months, hence the name Dōngqīng [Winter Green]. People in the Jiangdong region call it Dòngqīng [Frozen Green].

**[Collected Explanations]** Chen Cangqi states: The wood of Dongqing is white-grained, with markings suitable for carving into ivory-style tablet [xiangchi hu, i.e., the ceremonial tablet held at court]. Its leaves can be used to dye a crimson [fēi] color. Li Yong states: Dongqing grows on Mount Wutai; its leaves resemble those of the tree-of-heaven [chūn, Toona], its berries are red like those of the Chinese bush cherry [yùlǐ], slightly sour in flavor and hot in nature. This description differs somewhat from the above, suggesting there may be two distinct species called Dongqing.

Li Shizhen states: Dòngqīng is also a variety distinct from Nǚzhēn [Ligustrum]. It is occasionally found in the mountains. The distinguishing rule is: the one with slightly rounded leaves and red berries is Dòngqīng; the one with longer leaves and black berries is Nǚzhēn. According to the Jiuhuang Bencao [Materia Medica for Relief of Famines]: The Dòngqīng tree reaches a height of about one zhàng [≈3 m]; in form it resembles the holly-leaved osmanthus [gǔgǔzǐ tree, i.e., Ilex cornuta] but is exceedingly lush and full. Its leaves also resemble those of the hawthorn [zhāzǐ tree] but are smaller, and likewise resemble the leaves of the chūn [Toona] — slightly narrower, with a fairly rounded tip rather than pointed.

In the fifth month it bears small white flowers and sets berries about the size of beans, red in color. The tender young shoots may be deep-fried until cooked, then soaked in water to remove the bitter flavor, rinsed clean, seasoned with the five flavors, and eaten.

**Berries and Bark**

**[Flavor and Nature]** Sweet, bitter; cooling; non-toxic.

**[Principal Indications]**
— Steeped in wine, it dispels wind-deficiency [fēngxū] and nourishes and supplements the skin and flesh. The bark has the same efficacy. (Chen Cangqi)
— Burned to ash and incorporated into a facial ointment [miàngāo], it treats chapped and chilblained skin [jùnzhú], and is remarkably effective at eliminating scars and blemishes. (Su Song)

entities_mentioned.name: 冬青 / 凍青 (Dōngqīng / Dòngqīng)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ilex chinensis Sims or a closely related Ilex species (Chinese winterberry / Chinese holly)
entities_mentioned.notes: Li Shizhen distinguishes this plant from Nüzhen (Ligustrum lucidum) by its rounder leaves and red berries. The description — red berries, small white flowers in the fifth month, rounded leaves — aligns well with Ilex chinensis or similar red-berried Ilex spp.

entities_mentioned.notes: Contrasted here with Dongqing by its longer leaves and black berries.

entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a size/color comparison for the berries.

results.translator_notes: 1. 'Pijun zhu' (皸瘃) refers to skin conditions caused by cold: chapping (皸) and chilblains (瘃); translated accordingly. 2. 'Xiangchi hu' (象齒笏) literally means 'ivory-toothed tablet,' referring to the ceremonial ivory hu tablet carried by officials — the grain of the wood was likened to this. 3. The Jiuhuang Bencao (救荒本草, ca. 1406) is cited as a secondary source, consistent with Li Shizhen's method. 4. 'Fengxu' (風虛) is a classical TCM pathological category combining wind-evil intrusion and vacuity/deficiency; rendered as 'wind-deficiency.' 5. Li Yong (李邕) was a Tang dynasty calligrapher-official sometimes cited in pharmacopoeial contexts; the attribution here may refer to a now-lost botanical note by him.

results.original_text:
【釋名】鬼箭（《別錄》）、神箭。

時珍曰︰劉熙《釋名》言︰齊人謂箭羽為衛。此物幹有直羽，如箭羽、矛刃自衛之狀，故名。張揖《廣雅》謂之神箭，寇宗奭《衍義》言人家多燔之遣。

【集解】《別錄》曰︰衛矛生霍山山谷。八月採，陰乾。

普曰︰葉如桃，箭如羽，正月、二月、七月採，陰乾。或生田野。

弘景曰︰山野處處有之。削取皮、羽入藥，為用甚稀。

頌曰︰今江淮州郡亦或有之。三月以後生莖，莖長四、五尺許。其幹有三羽，狀如箭翎羽。葉似山茶，青色。八月、十一月、十二月採條莖，陰乾。其木亦名狗骨。

宗奭曰︰所在山谷皆有，平陸未嘗見也。葉絕少。其莖黃褐色，若柏皮，三面如鋒刃。

時珍曰︰鬼箭生山石間，小株成叢。春長嫩條，條上四面有羽如箭羽，視之若三羽爾。青葉狀似野茶，對生，味酸澀。三、四月開碎花，黃綠色。結實大如冬青子。山人不識，惟樵采之。

曰︰凡使勿用石茆，根頭真相似，只是上葉不同，味各別耳。

【氣味】苦，寒，無毒。普曰︰神農、黃帝、桐君︰苦，無毒。大明曰︰甘，澀。權曰︰有小毒。

【主治】女子崩中下血，腹滿汗出，除邪，殺鬼毒蠱疰（《本經》）。中惡腹痛，去白蟲，消皮膚風毒腫，令陰中解（《別錄》）。療婦人血氣，大效（馬志）。破陳血，能落胎，主百邪鬼魅（甄權）。通月經，破症結。

【發明】頌曰︰古方崔氏療惡疰在心，痛不可忍，有鬼箭羽湯；暴心痛，或中惡氣毒痛，大黃湯亦用之，並大方也。見《外台秘要》、《千金》諸書中。

曰︰凡婦人產後血暈血結，血聚於胸中，或偏於少腹，或連於脅肋者。四物湯四兩，倍當歸，加鬼箭、紅花、玄胡索各一兩，為末，煎服。
results.translation:
**[Explanatory Names]** Guǐ Jiàn [Ghost Arrow] (Bielu); Shén Jiàn [Spirit Arrow].

Li Shizhen states: Liu Xi's Shiming [Explaining Names] says that the people of Qi called arrow fletching wèi [guard/protect]. This plant's stems bear straight wing-like ridges resembling arrow fletching and spear blades used for self-protection, hence the name. Zhang Ji's Guangya calls it Shén Jiàn [Spirit Arrow]. Kou Zongshi's Yanyi notes that households often burn it to drive away [evil].

**[Collected Commentaries]** The Bielu states: Wèi Máo grows in the mountain valleys of Huoshan. It is collected in the eighth month and dried in the shade.

Pu [Wu Pu] states: Its leaves resemble those of the peach; its stems bear wing-like ridges like arrow fletching. Collected in the first, second, and seventh months, and dried in the shade. It may also grow in fields and open country.

Hongjing [Tao Hongjing] states: It is found everywhere in mountain wilds. The bark and wing-ridges are scraped off for medicinal use, though they are employed very rarely.

Song [Su Song] states: It is also found in the prefectures of Jianghuai. After the third month the stems grow to about four or five chi in length. The stems bear three wing-ridges resembling arrow-feather fletching. The leaves resemble those of mountain camellia [Camellia japonica] and are green. The stem branches are collected in the eighth, eleventh, and twelfth months and dried in the shade. This wood is also called Gǒu Gǔ [Dog Bone].

Zongshi [Kou Zongshi] states: It is found in all mountain valleys but has never been seen on flat plains. It has very few leaves. The stem is yellow-brown in color, resembling cypress bark, with three sides like sharp blades.

Li Shizhen states: Guǐ Jiàn grows among mountain rocks in small clumping shrubs. In spring it puts forth tender new shoots; on all four sides of the shoots are wing-like ridges resembling arrow fletching, though to the eye they appear to number three. The green leaves resemble wild tea, grow in opposite pairs, and have an astringent, sour flavor. In the third and fourth months small flowers open, yellow-green in color. The fruits are about the size of holly [Ilex] berries. Mountain folk do not recognize it and collect it only as firewood.

[An authority] states: When using this drug, do not substitute Shí Máo [stone sedge / Eriophorum or similar]; the root heads look very similar, but the upper leaves differ and the flavors are distinct.

**[Qi and Flavor]** Bitter, cold, non-toxic. Pu states: According to Shennong, Huangdi, and Tongjun — bitter, non-toxic. Daming states: Sweet and astringent. Quan states: Slightly toxic.

**[Principal Indications / Treatments]**
— Flooding and spotting [uterine hemorrhage] in women, abdominal fullness with sweating; expels evil influences, kills ghost-toxins and gu-possession [Benjing].
— Abdominal pain from malign influences, expels white worms [intestinal parasites], disperses wind-toxic swellings of the skin, and relieves constriction of the vaginal region [Bielu].
— Highly effective for treating women's blood and qi disorders [Ma Zhi].
— Breaks up stagnant old blood, can induce abortion, and governs all evil-spirit afflictions and demonic possession [Zhen Quan].
— Opens the menses, breaks up concretions and accumulations [zheng jie].

**[Elucidation]** Su Song states: Among the ancient formulas, the Cui family formula treats severe malign-possession pain in the heart that is unbearable — there is the Guǐ Jiàn Yǔ Tāng [Ghost Arrow Feather Decoction]; for sudden heart pain or pain from malign-qi poisoning, the Dà Huáng Tāng [Rhubarb Decoction] also uses it — both are major formulas, found in the Waitai Miyao and the Qianjin and other books.

[An authority] states: For women after childbirth suffering from blood-dizziness and blood-binding — blood accumulating in the chest, or lodged in the lower abdomen, or extending to the flanks and ribs — use Sìwù Tāng [Four Substances Decoction] at four liang, doubling the portion of Dāng Guī [Angelica sinensis], and adding Guǐ Jiàn [wèi máo winged bark], Hóng Huā [safflower], and Xuán Hú Suǒ [Corydalis yanhusuo] at one liang each; grind to powder and take as a decoction.

entities_mentioned.name: 衛矛 (Wèi Máo)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Euonymus alatus (winged spindle tree) — the winged bark/stem material
entities_mentioned.notes: Also called 鬼箭 (Guǐ Jiàn, 'ghost arrow') and 神箭 (Shén Jiàn, 'spirit arrow'). The corky wing-like ridges on the stems are the primary medicinal part used (known as 鬼箭羽, guǐ jiàn yǔ).

entities_mentioned.name: 石茆 (Shí Máo)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Eriophorum species (cotton-grass sedge) or another sedge; exact identification uncertain
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned as an adulterant with similar-looking root heads but different leaves and flavor. Not to be substituted for wèi máo.

entities_mentioned.name: 四物湯 (Sìwù Tāng)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical TCM formula: Four Substances Decoction (Rehmannia, Angelica sinensis, Ligusticum chuanxiong, Paeonia)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a base formula for postpartum blood disorders, with wèi máo, safflower, and Corydalis added.

entities_mentioned.name: 當歸 (Dāng Guī)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Angelica sinensis
entities_mentioned.notes: Doubled in the postpartum formula.

entities_mentioned.name: 紅花 (Hóng Huā)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Carthamus tinctorius (safflower)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used at one liang in the postpartum blood-stagnation formula.

entities_mentioned.name: 玄胡索 (Xuán Hú Suǒ)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Corydalis yanhusuo (yanhusuo)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used at one liang; a standard blood-moving, pain-relieving herb in TCM.

entities_mentioned.name: 大黃 (Dà Huáng)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rheum palmatum or related Rheum species (Chinese rhubarb root)
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced in the Dà Huáng Tāng formula for malign-qi abdominal pain.

results.translator_notes:
1. The text attributed to 'Pu' (普) refers to Wu Pu (吳普), author of the early materia medica Wupǔ Bencao. 'Hongjing' (弘景) is Tao Hongjing (陶弘景). 'Song' (頌) is Su Song (蘇頌). 'Zongshi' (宗奭) is Kou Zongshi (寇宗奭). 'Quan' (權) is Zhen Quan (甄權). 'Daming' (大明) refers to the Rihuazi Bencao compiler.
2. The attribution '曰︰凡使勿用石茆…' is anonymous in the source as given — likely a formulary or processing note (paozhi convention) possibly from Lei Xiao (雷斅); the original may be corrupt or the attribution lost due to OCR truncation.
3. 'Flooding and spotting' (崩中下血) refers to uterine hemorrhage. 'Gu-possession' (蠱疰) is a traditional category of parasitic or malign-influence disease.
4. 'Ghost-toxins' (鬼毒) and 'demonic possession' (鬼魅) reflect the demonological disease categories prevalent in classical Chinese medicine.
5. The sentence ending 'Kou Zongshi's Yanyi notes that households often burn it to drive away [evil]' appears truncated in the source (ending mid-sentence at 遣); the meaning is inferred from context and parallel sources indicating households burned guǐ jiàn to dispel ghosts.
6. The postpartum formula attribution is also anonymous in the source text as reproduced; it may derive from a later compiler's addition.

【釋名】芸香（音雲）、花（音七里香。香馥，故名。

按︰周必大云︰柘音陣，出《南史》。荊俗訛柘為鄭，呼為鄭礬，而江南又訛鄭為也。黃庭堅云︰江南野中碇花極多。野人採葉燒灰，以染紫為黝，不借礬而成。子因以易其名為山礬。

【集解】時珍曰︰山礬生江、淮、湖、蜀野中。樹之大者，株高丈生不對節，光澤堅強，略有齒，凌冬不凋。三月開花繁白，如雪六出，黃蕊甚芬香。結子大如椒，青黑色，熟則黃色，可食。其葉味澀，人取以染黃及收豆腐，或雜入茗中。按︰沈括《筆談》云︰古人藏書辟蠹用芸香，謂之芸草，即今之七里香也。葉類豌豆，作小叢生，啜嗅之極芬香。秋間葉上微白如粉污，辟蠹殊驗。又按︰《蒼頡解詁》云︰芸香似邪蒿，可食，辟紙蠹。許慎《說文》云︰芸，似苜蓿。成公綏《芸香賦》云︰莖類秋竹，枝象青松。郭義恭《廣志》有芸香膠。《杜陽編》云︰芸香，草也，出于闐國。其香潔白如玉，入土不朽。元載造芸暉堂，以此為屑塗壁也。據此數說，則芸香非一種。沈氏指為七里香者，不知何據？所云葉類豌豆，啜嗅芬香，秋間有粉者，亦與今之七里香不相類，狀頗似烏藥葉，恐沈氏亦自臆度爾。曾端伯以七里香為玉蕊花，未知的否？

葉

【氣味】酸、澀、微甘，無毒。

【主治】久痢，止渴，殺蚤、蠹。用三十片，同老薑三片，浸水蒸熱，洗爛弦風眼（時珍）。
results.translation:
**Names and Nomenclature [釋名]**

Also called Yunxiang [芸香, pronounced 'yún'], and Qilixiang [七里香, 'seven-league fragrance']. It is so named for its rich, pervasive fragrance.

Zhou Bida notes: the character 柘 is pronounced 'zhèn' [阵], as attested in the *Nan Shi* [History of the Southern Dynasties]. In the Jing region [Hubei area], common speech corrupted 柘 into 鄭, so it was called 'Zhengfan' [鄭礬]; then in Jiangnan it was further corrupted from 鄭 into 礬. Huang Tingjian [the Song poet] says: 'In the wilds of Jiangnan there are extremely many dìng-flowers [碇花]. Country people gather the leaves, burn them to ash, and use the ash to dye purple cloth to a dark hue — achieving this without actual alum [礬].' He therefore changed its name to Shanfan [山礬, 'mountain alum'].

**Collected Commentaries [集解]**

Li Shizhen states: Shanfan grows wild in Jiang [Jiangxi/Jiangsu], Huai [Huai River region], Hu [Hunan/Hubei], and Shu [Sichuan]. The larger specimens reach a height of one zhang [~3 m]. The leaves do not grow in opposite pairs at the nodes; they are glossy, firm, and slightly toothed, and do not drop in winter. In the third month [April] the tree blooms profusely in white flowers resembling snow — six-petaled with yellow stamens — and they are extremely fragrant. The fruit is the size of pepper berries, bluish-black when unripe, turning yellow when ripe, and is edible. The leaves have an astringent flavor; people use them to dye cloth yellow, to coagulate tofu, or mix them into tea.

Shen Kuo's *Mengxi Bitan* [Dream Pool Essays] states: The ancients stored books using yunxiang [芸香] to repel bookworms; this herb was called 'yuncao' and is the same as today's qilixiang [七里香]. Its leaves resemble those of the pea plant; it grows in small clumps and is extremely fragrant when sniffed. In autumn, the leaves develop a slight whitish powdery coating, which is remarkably effective at repelling bookworms.

The *Cangjie Jiegu* [Glosses on Cangjie's Characters] states: yunxiang resembles wild wormwood [邪蒿], is edible, and repels paper-eating insects. Xu Shen's *Shuowen Jiezi* states: yun [芸] resembles alfalfa [苜蓿]. Cheng Gongsui's 'Ode to Yunxiang' describes: 'Stems like autumn bamboo, branches like green pine.' Guo Yigong's *Guangzhi* [Comprehensive Records] mentions a yunxiang resin [芸香膠]. The *Duyang Bian* states: yunxiang is a grass from the kingdom of Khotan [于闐]. Its fragrance is as pure and white as jade; buried in the earth it does not rot. Yuan Zai [a Tang official] constructed the Yunhui Hall and plastered its walls with powdered yunxiang.

Given all these accounts, it is clear that 'yunxiang' is not a single plant. On what basis Shen Kuo identified it as qilixiang is unknown. What he describes — leaves resembling pea leaves, intensely fragrant when sniffed, with a powdery white coating in autumn — does not match today's qilixiang at all; it rather resembles the leaves of wuyao [乌药, *Lindera aggregata*]. It seems Shen Kuo was merely guessing. Zeng Duanbo identified qilixiang as the yuyrui flower [玉蕊花]; whether this is correct is also uncertain.

**Leaves**

**Flavor and Nature [氣味]:** Sour, astringent, slightly sweet; non-toxic.

**Indications and Uses [主治]:** Chronic dysentery; quenching thirst; killing fleas and bookworms. For treating chronic weeping wind-eye [爛弦風眼, blepharitis with chronic discharge]: take thirty leaves together with three slices of aged ginger, soak in water, steam until hot, and wash the affected eyes. (Li Shizhen)

The term 爛弦風眼 refers to a classical ophthalmological condition combining chronic eyelid margin inflammation (blepharitis) with wind-pathogen involvement, producing weeping, crusting, and erosion of the lid margins. 3. 'Six-petaled' (六出) is the classic poetic description for snow-like flowers and here describes the flower form of Symplocos. 4. The phrase 不借礬而成 ('achieved without borrowing alum') is the etymological explanation for why Huang Tingjian renamed the plant 山礬 — the ash alone fixes dye colors that normally require alum as a mordant.

而又以甘州者為絕品。今陝之蘭州、靈州、九原以西枸杞，並是大樹，其葉濃根粗。河西及甘州者，其子圓如櫻桃，曝乾緊小少核，乾亦紅潤甘美，味如葡萄，可作果食，異於他處者。

沈存中《筆談》亦言：陝西極邊生者高丈餘，大可作柱。葉長數寸，無刺。根皮如濃朴。則入藥大抵以河西者為上也。《種樹書》言：收子及掘根種於肥壤中，待苗生，剪為蔬食，甚佳。

【氣味】枸杞：苦，寒，無毒。

《別錄》曰：根：大寒。子：微寒，無毒。冬采根，春、夏採葉，秋采莖、實。權曰：枸杞：甘，平。子、葉同。

宗奭曰：枸杞當用梗皮，地骨當用根皮，子當用紅實。其皮寒，根大寒，子微寒。今人多用其子為補腎藥，是未曾考竟經意，當量其虛實冷熱用之。

時珍曰：今考《本經》只云枸杞，不指是根、莖、葉、子。《別錄》乃增根大寒、子微寒字，似以枸杞為苗。而甄氏《藥性論》乃云枸杞甘、平，子、葉皆同，似以枸杞為根；寇氏《衍義》又以枸杞為梗皮，皆是臆說。按：陶弘景言枸杞根、實為服食家用。西河女子服枸杞法，根、莖、葉、花、實俱采用。則《本經》所列氣味主治，蓋通根、苗、花、實而言，初無分別也。後世以枸杞子為滋補藥，地骨皮為退熱藥，始歧而二之。竊謂枸杞苗葉味苦甘而氣涼，根味甘淡氣寒，子味甘氣平。氣味既殊，則功用當別。此後人發前人未到之處者也。

苗：苦，寒。權曰：甘，平。時珍曰：甘，涼。伏砒、砂。
根（地骨皮）：苦，寒。《別錄》曰：大寒。權曰：甘，平。時珍曰：甘、淡，寒。杲曰：苦，平、寒。升也，陰也。好古曰：入足少陰、手少陽經。製硫黃、丹砂。
子：苦，寒。權曰：甘，平。

【主治】枸杞主五內邪氣，熱中消渴，周痹風濕。久服，堅筋骨，輕身不老，耐寒暑（《本經》）。下胸脅氣，客熱頭痛，補內傷大勞噓吸，強陰，利大小腸（《別錄》）。補精氣諸不足，易顏色，變白，明目安神，令人長壽（甄權）。
除煩益志，補五勞七傷，壯心氣，去皮膚骨節間風，消熱毒，散瘡腫（大明）。和羊肉作羹，益人，除風明目。作飲代茶，止渴，消熱煩，益陽事，解面毒，與乳酪相惡。汁注目中，去風障赤膜昏痛（甄權）。去上焦心肺客熱（時珍）。

地骨皮
細銼，拌面煮熟，吞之，去腎家風，益精氣（甄權）。去骨熱消渴（孟詵）。解骨蒸肌熱消渴，風濕痹，堅筋骨，涼血（元素）。治在表無定之風邪，傳屍有汗之骨蒸（李杲）。瀉腎火，降肺中伏火，去胞中火，退熱，補正氣（好古）。治上膈吐血。煎湯嗽口，止齒血，治骨槽風（吳瑞）。治金瘡神驗（陳承）。去下焦肝腎虛熱（時珍）。

枸杞子
堅筋骨，耐老，除風，去虛勞，補精氣（孟詵）。主心病嗌乾心痛，渴而引飲；腎病消中（好古）。滋腎潤肺。榨油點燈，明目（時珍）。

【發明】時珍曰：此乃通指枸杞根、苗、花、實並用之功也。其單用之功，今列於下。

苗
弘景曰：枸杞葉作羹，小苦。俗諺云：去家千里，勿食蘿摩、枸杞。此言二物補益精氣，強盛陰道也。枸杞根、實為服食家用，其說甚美，名為仙人之杖，遠有旨乎？

【釋名】巨骨（《別錄》）。

【集解】《別錄》曰︰溲疏生熊耳川谷，及田野故丘墟地。四月採。當之曰︰溲疏一名楊櫨，一名牡荊，一名空疏。皮白中空，時時有節。子似枸杞子，冬月熟，赤色，味甘苦。末代乃無識者。此非人籬垣之楊櫨也。

恭曰︰溲疏，形似空疏，樹高丈許，白皮。其子八、九月熟，赤色，似枸杞，必兩兩相對，味苦，與空疏不同。空疏即楊櫨，其子為莢，不似溲疏。

志曰︰溲疏、枸杞雖則相似，然溲疏有刺，枸杞無刺，以此為別。

頌曰︰溲疏亦有巨骨之名，如枸杞之名地骨，當亦相類。方書鮮用，宜細辨之。

機曰︰按︰李當之但言溲疏子似枸杞子，不曾言樹相似。馬志因其子相似，遂謂樹亦相似，以有刺、無刺為別。蘇頌又因巨骨、地骨之名，疑其相類。殊不知枸杞未嘗無刺，但小則刺多，大則刺少耳。本草中異物同名甚多，況一骨字之同耶？以此為言，尤見穿鑿。

時珍曰︰汪機所斷似矣，而自亦不能的指為何物也。

【氣味】辛，寒，無毒。《別錄》曰︰苦，微寒。

之才曰︰漏盧為之使。

【主治】皮膚中熱，除邪氣，止遺溺，利水道（《本經》）。

除胃中熱，下氣。可作浴湯（《別錄》）。時珍曰︰按︰孫真人《千金方》，治婦人下焦三十六疾，承……
results.translation:
**[Names and Nomenclature]**
Also known as Jugu [巨骨, 'Giant Bone'] (according to the *Bielu* [*Supplementary Records of Famous Physicians*]).

**[Collected Commentaries]**
The *Bielu* states: Soushu [溲疏] grows in the river valleys of Xiong'er [熊耳Mountain] and in wild fields and abandoned ruins. It is harvested in the fourth month.

Li Dangzhi states: Soushu is also known by the names Yanglu [楊櫨], Mujing [牡荊], and Kongshu [空疏]. Its bark is white and its interior hollow, with nodes at intervals. Its berries resemble those of wolfberry [枸杞], ripen in winter, are red in color, and taste sweet-bitter. In later ages there were none who could identify it. This is not the Yanglu [楊櫨] planted along garden fences.

Su Gong [恭] states: Soushu resembles Kongshu in form; the tree grows to about one zhang in height and has white bark. Its berries ripen in the eighth or ninth month; they are red and resemble wolfberries, and always grow in pairs opposite one another. The taste is bitter, which distinguishes it from Kongshu. Kongshu is in fact the same as Yanglu, and its fruits form pods, unlike those of Soushu.

Ma Zhi [志] states: Although Soushu and wolfberry [枸杞] are similar, Soushu has thorns while wolfberry does not — this is how to tell them apart.

Su Song [頌] states: Soushu also bears the name Jugu ['Giant Bone'], just as wolfberry is named Digu ['Earth Bone']; the two may therefore be related. Soushu is rarely used in medical formularies, and should be carefully distinguished.

Wang Ji [機] states: Upon examination, Li Dangzhi only said that the berries of Soushu resemble those of wolfberry; he never said the trees themselves are similar. Ma Zhi, because the fruits are similar, concluded the trees must also be similar, using the presence or absence of thorns to distinguish them. Su Song then, because of the shared character 'bone' [骨] in their names Jugu and Digu, speculated they were related. Yet it should be noted that wolfberry is not entirely without thorns — small plants have many thorns, large ones fewer. In the materia medica, different plants sharing the same name is very common; how much more so when only a single character — 'bone' — is shared? To reason on this basis is especially forced and contrived.

Li Shizhen [時珍] states: Wang Ji's conclusion seems reasonable, yet he himself was unable to definitively identify what plant this actually is.

**[Flavor and Nature]**
Acrid [辛], cold [寒], non-toxic. The *Bielu* states: bitter [苦], slightly cold [微寒].

Zheng Quan [之才] states: Loulu [漏盧, *Rhaponticum uniflorum*] acts as its guiding herb [使].

**[Indications and Treatments]**
Treats heat in the skin, eliminates pathogenic [邪] qi, stops urinary incontinence [遺溺], and promotes urination [利水道] (according to the *Bencao Jing* [*Classic of Materia Medica*]).

Eliminates heat in the stomach, descends qi [下氣]. Can be prepared as a medicated bath [浴湯] (according to the *Bielu*). Li Shizhen notes: According to Sun Simiao's [孫真人] *Qianjin Fang* [*Essential Prescriptions Worth a Thousand Gold*], for treating the thirty-six diseases of the lower burner [下焦] in women, [text continues, passage breaks off here]…

entities_mentioned.name: 溲疏 (Soushu)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Deutzia scabra or a related Deutzia species (family Hydrangeaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: The identity of Soushu was disputed even in Li Shizhen's time. Some scholars identify it with Deutzia spp.; others have suggested Philadelphus or related shrubs. The paired red berries and white-barked hollow stems are characteristic details. Li Shizhen himself admitted the plant could not be definitively identified.

entities_mentioned.name: 巨骨 (Jugu)
entities_mentioned.notes: Alternate name for Soushu recorded in the Bielu. The 'bone' character was noted to parallel the wolfberry's alternate name Digu (地骨, 'Earth Bone'), but Li Shizhen's commentator Wang Ji cautioned against over-interpreting this nominal similarity.

entities_mentioned.name: 楊櫨 (Yanglu)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Cotinus coggygria (smoke tree) or a Lonicera species; uncertain
entities_mentioned.notes: Listed as a synonym for both Soushu (by Li Dangzhi) and Kongshu (by Su Gong), causing significant confusion. Its fruits were described as pods, distinguishing it from Soushu.

entities_mentioned.name: 空疏 (Kongshu)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Lonicera or a related hollow-stemmed shrub; uncertain
entities_mentioned.notes: Equated with Yanglu by Su Gong. Described as having pod-type fruits, unlike Soushu's berry-like fruits.

entities_mentioned.name: 枸杞 (Gouqi / Wolfberry)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lycium chinense or Lycium barbarum (Solanaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used repeatedly as a comparator plant to describe Soushu's berries. Its root bark is known as Digu (地骨皮).

entities_mentioned.name: 漏盧 (Loulu)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rhaponticum uniflorum (Asteraceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Listed as the guiding herb [使藥] for Soushu.

entities_mentioned.name: 牡荊 (Mujing)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vitex negundo var. cannabifolia (Lamiaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Listed by Li Dangzhi as a synonym for Soushu, though this is likely erroneous; Mujing is generally well-identified as a Vitex species.

results.translator_notes:
1. The segment ends abruptly mid-sentence in the 【主治】section, indicating truncation of the source text. The passage from Sun Simiao's Qianjin Fang concerning the 'thirty-six diseases of the lower burner in women' is incomplete.
2. The phrase '末代乃無識者' rendered as 'in later ages there were none who could identify it' reflects a lament about the loss of botanical knowledge over time — a recurring theme in the Bencao Gangmu.
3. Wang Ji's critique of Su Song's reasoning (regarding shared name-characters as evidence of botanical relationship) is an early example of philological caution in Chinese natural history scholarship.
4. The OCR text has '四月採當之曰' which appears to conflate two separate statements: '四月採' (harvested in the fourth month) and '當之曰' (Li Dangzhi states). These have been separated in the cleaned text.

【Collected Explanations】 Su Gong states: Yanglu [楊櫨], also known as Kongshu [空疏], grows everywhere, commonly found growing along fences and walls. Its fruits are pod-like [莢].

Leaves

【Qi and Flavor】 Bitter; cold; toxic.

【Indications and Treatment】 For carbuncles [疽] and malignant sores [惡瘡]: boil the leaves in water and wash the affected area with the decoction; recovery is swift. (From the Tang Bencao [《唐本》].)

Wood Ear [木耳]: See the Vegetables section [菜部].

entities_mentioned.name: 楊櫨 (Yanglu)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cotinus coggygria (Smoke Tree / Smoke Bush, family Anacardiaceae); alternatively sometimes associated with Rhus or related genera
entities_mentioned.notes: Also called 空疏 (Kongshu). Described as growing along fences and walls, with pod-like fruits. The synonym 空疏 has also been applied to Sambucus species in some sources, creating some taxonomic ambiguity.

entities_mentioned.name: 木耳 (Wood Ear fungus)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Auricularia auricula-judae (Wood Ear / Jelly Ear fungus)
entities_mentioned.notes: Only cross-referenced here; the full entry appears in the Vegetables (菜部) section of the Bencao Gangmu.

results.translator_notes: The ailment 疽惡瘡 refers to deep abscesses or carbuncles combined with malignant or putrid sores — a common pairing in classical Chinese medical texts. The preparation method is an external wash (外洗法): the leaves are boiled in water and the resulting decoction is applied topically, not taken internally. Su Gong (蘇恭) is the Tang dynasty pharmacologist credited as the compiler of the Tang Bencao (新修本草, 659 CE), cited here as 《唐本》.

【釋名】風藥。

時珍曰：生於石間向陽之處，故名石南。桂陽呼為風藥，充茗及浸酒飲能愈頭風，故名。

按：《范石湖集》云：修江出欒茶，治頭風。今南人無所謂欒茶者，豈即此乎？

【集解】《別錄》曰：石南生華陰山谷。三月、四月採葉，八月採實。

弘景曰：今東間皆有之，葉如枇杷葉。方用亦稀。

恭曰：葉似莽草，凌冬不凋。關中者葉細為好。江山以南者，葉長大如枇杷葉，無氣味，殊不任用。保升曰：終南斜谷有石處甚饒。今市人以石葦為之，誤矣。頌曰：今南北皆有之。生於石上，株極有高碩者。江湖間出者，葉如枇杷，上有小刺，凌冬不凋。春生白花成簇，秋結細紅實。關隴間出者，葉似莽草，青黃色，背有紫點，雨多則並生，長及二、三寸。根橫，細紫色。無花實，葉至茂密。南北人多移植亭院間，陰翳可愛，不透日氣。入藥以關中葉細者為良。魏王《花木志》云：南方石南樹野生。二月開花，連著實。實如燕覆子，八月熟。民采取核，和魚羹尤美。今無用者。宗奭曰：石南葉似枇杷葉之小者，而背無毛，光而不皺。正、二月間開花。冬有二葉為花苞，苞既開，中有十五余花，大小如椿花，甚細碎。每一苞約彈許大，成一球。一花六葉，一朵有七、八球，淡白綠色，葉末微淡赤色。花既開，蕊滿花，但見蕊不見花。花才罷，去年綠葉盡脫落，漸生新葉。京洛、河北、河東、山東頗少，人故少用。湖南北、江東西、二浙甚多，故人多用。

葉

【氣味】辛、苦，平，有毒。

之才曰：五加皮為使。惡小薊。

【主治】養腎氣，內傷陰衰，利筋骨皮毛（《本經》）。可久服，令思男（《別錄》）。能添腎氣，治軟腳煩悶疼，殺蟲，逐諸風（甄權）。浸酒飲，治頭風（時珍）。殺蠱毒，破積聚，逐風痺。

【發明】恭曰：石南葉為療風邪丸散之要，今醫家不復用其實矣。

權曰：雖能養腎，亦令人陰痿。

時珍曰：古方為治風痺腎弱要藥。今人絕不知用，識者亦少，蓋由甄氏《藥性論》有令陰痿之說也。殊不知服此藥者，能令腎強，嗜欲之人藉此放恣，以致痿弱，歸咎於藥，良可慨也。毛文錫《茶譜》云：湘人四月採楊桐草，搗汁浸水蒸，作為飯食；必采石南芽為茶飲，乃去風也。暑月尤宜。楊桐即南燭也。
results.translation:
**[Explanation of Names]** Wind Medicine [feng yao].

Li Shizhen states: It grows among rocks in sun-facing places, hence the name Shi Nan [Stone South/Southern]. In Guiyang it is called Feng Yao [Wind Medicine]; used as a tea or steeped in wine it can cure head-wind [migraine/headache disorders], hence that name.

Note: The *Collected Works of Fan Shihu* states: "The Xiu River produces Luan Cha [Luan Tea], which treats head-wind." Southerners today know of no such thing as Luan Cha — could it perhaps be this very plant?

**[Collected Commentaries]**
*Mingyi Bielu* states: Shi Nan grows in the mountain valleys of Huayin. Leaves are gathered in the third and fourth months; fruit is gathered in the eighth month.

Tao Hongjing states: It is found throughout the eastern regions; its leaves resemble those of the loquat [pipa, *Eriobotrya japonica*]. It is rarely used in prescriptions.

Su Gong states: The leaves resemble those of Mang Cao [*Illicium lanceolatum*] and do not wither in winter. The variety from the Guan[zhong] region, with its finer leaves, is the best. The variety from south of the Jiangshan range has large leaves like loquat leaves, has no fragrance or taste, and is quite unsuitable for use. [Han] Baosheng states: It grows abundantly in rocky areas of the Zhongnan and Xie valleys. Market vendors today use Shi Wei [*Pyrrosia* sp.] as a substitute — this is an error. Su Song states: It is now found in both the north and the south. It grows on rocks and some specimens grow quite tall and large. The variety from the Jianghu region has leaves like loquat with small spines, and does not wither in winter. In spring it produces clusters of white flowers; in autumn it bears small red fruit. The variety from the Guanlong region has leaves resembling Mang Cao, of a blue-yellow color, with purple spots on the underside; when rain is plentiful, leaves grow side by side reaching two to three cun in length. The roots grow horizontally and are fine and purple in color. This variety produces no flowers or fruit, but its foliage is exceedingly dense. People throughout north and south commonly transplant it into garden pavilions and courtyards, where its shade is lovely and it blocks out the sunlight. For medicinal use, the Guanzhong variety with its fine leaves is considered superior. The Wei King's *Record of Flowers and Trees* states: "In the south, the Shi Nan tree grows wild. It blooms in the second month and simultaneously sets fruit. The fruit resembles that of the Yan Fu Zi [unidentified climbing plant]; it ripens in the eighth month. The people gather its seeds and add them to fish stew, which is especially delicious." Today this use is no longer practiced. Kou Zongshi states: The leaves of Shi Nan resemble smaller loquat leaves, but are hairless on the underside, smooth and not wrinkled. It flowers in the first and second months. In winter two leaves form a flower bud; when the bud opens it contains more than fifteen flowers, of a size similar to ailanthus [chun] flowers — very small and delicate. Each bud is roughly the size of a pellet [dan], forming a single ball. Each flower has six petals; one cluster [duo] has seven or eight balls, of a pale whitish-green color with the leaf tips faintly reddish. Once in full bloom, the stamens fill the flower so that only the stamens are visible, not the petals. As soon as flowering ends, all the previous year's green leaves fall off and new leaves gradually emerge. The plant is quite scarce in Jinglo, Hebei, Hedong, and Shandong, and thus rarely used there. It is very abundant in Hunan, Hubei, Jiangdong, Jiangxi, and the two Zhe [Zhejiang] regions, and so is used more widely there.

---
**Leaves**

**[Qi and Flavor]** Acrid [xin], bitter [ku]; balanced [ping]; toxic.

[Xu] Zhicai states: Wu Jia Pi [Acanthopanax bark] acts as its conductor [shi]. It is antagonistic to Xiao Ji [small thistle, *Cirsium setosum*].

**[Indications and Treatments]**
- Nourishes kidney qi; [treats] internal injury and decline of yin; benefits the sinews, bones, skin, and hair. (*Shennong Bencao Jing*)
- May be taken over a long period; causes [women to] long for men [i.e., increases female libido]. (*Mingyi Bielu*)
- Can replenish kidney qi; treats weakness of the legs [ruan jiao], vexation, oppression, and pain; kills worms; dispels all manner of wind. (Zhen Quan)
- Steeped in wine and drunk, treats head-wind. (Li Shizhen)
- Kills gu toxin [parasite-based poison]; breaks up accumulations and gatherings [ji ju]; dispels wind-impediment [feng bi].

**[Elucidation]**
Su Gong states: Shi Nan leaf is an essential ingredient in pills and powders for treating wind evil. Physicians today no longer use the fruit.

Zhen Quan states: Although it can nourish the kidneys, it also causes erectile dysfunction [yin wei] in men.

Li Shizhen states: In ancient formulas this was a key medicinal for treating wind-impediment and kidney weakness. People today do not use it at all, and few recognize it — this is largely because Zhen Shi's *Yaoxing Lun* [Treatise on the Properties of Medicinals] contains the statement that it causes erectile dysfunction. Yet what those who use this medicine do not understand is that it strengthens the kidneys; those with excessive desires use the drug as a license for indulgence, thereby bringing about weakness themselves, and then blame the medicine — this is truly lamentable. Mao Wenxi's *Cha Pu* [Tea Manual] states: "The people of Xiang [Hunan] gather Yang Tong Cao [*Eurya japonica*] in the fourth month, pound it into juice, soak water, and steam it into a food; they invariably also gather Shi Nan buds to brew as a tea — this dispels wind. It is especially suitable in summer." Yang Tong is the same as Nan Zhu [*Vaccinium bracteatum*].

entities_mentioned.name: 石南 (Shi Nan)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Photinia serrulata (Chinese photinia) or possibly Photinia davidiana; some sources also equate it with Prunus serrulata in older texts, but Photinia is the consensus
entities_mentioned.notes: Multiple regional varieties described with notably different morphology; the Guanzhong variety with small leaves resembling Mang Cao may be a different species from the Jianghu variety with loquat-like leaves.

entities_mentioned.name: 枇杷 (Pi Pa)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Eriobotrya japonica (loquat)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a morphological reference for leaf shape.

entities_mentioned.name: 莽草 (Mang Cao)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Illicium lanceolatum (lance-leaf star anise / poisonous anise)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a morphological comparison for the Guanzhong variety of Shi Nan.

entities_mentioned.name: 石葦 (Shi Wei)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pyrrosia spp. (felt ferns)
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned as an erroneous market substitute for Shi Nan.

entities_mentioned.name: 五加皮 (Wu Jia Pi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Eleutherococcus gracilistylus / Acanthopanax gracilistylus bark
entities_mentioned.notes: Named as the conductor herb (shi) for Shi Nan.

entities_mentioned.name: 小薊 (Xiao Ji)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cirsium setosum (field thistle / setose thistle)
entities_mentioned.notes: Herb antagonistic to Shi Nan; should not be used together.

entities_mentioned.name: 楊桐草 / 楊桐 (Yang Tong Cao)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Eurya japonica or Cleyera japonica
entities_mentioned.notes: Equated by Li Shizhen with Nan Zhu (南燭, Vaccinium bracteatum); scholarly opinion varies on exact identification.

entities_mentioned.name: 南燭 (Nan Zhu)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vaccinium bracteatum (rice-steaming vaccinium / Chinese blueberry)
entities_mentioned.notes: Li Shizhen states Yang Tong is the same as Nan Zhu.

entities_mentioned.name: 欒茶 (Luan Cha)
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned in Fan Shihu's collected works as a tea from the Xiu River that treats head-wind; Li Shizhen speculates it may be Shi Nan but leaves the question open.

results.translator_notes:
1. The source text has a lacuna after '豈即' (likely '此乎' or similar closing phrase); the reconstructed reading '此乎？' is supplied from context and noted.
2. Several passages in 集解 appear to have minor OCR issues (e.g., '葉似 草' for '葉似莽草'); these have been silently corrected where unambiguous.
3. '令思男' in 別錄 is a notable pharmacological-sexual claim — literally 'causes [one] to think of men,' interpreted as increasing female sexual desire in classical pharmacological commentary.
4. The claim about erectile dysfunction (陰痿) and Li Shizhen's rebuttal is a significant passage in the history of Chinese pharmacological reasoning about secondary versus primary drug effects.
5. '燕覆子' in the Wei King's Flora citation is unidentified; it may refer to the fruit of a climbing or creeping plant but cannot be confidently matched to a modern species.

【釋名】黃荊（《圖經》）、小荊（《本經》）、弘景曰︰既是牡荊，不應有子。小荊應是牡荊。牡荊子大於蔓荊子，而反呼小荊，恐以樹形為言。不知蔓荊樹亦高碩也。

恭曰︰牡荊作樹，不為蔓生，故稱為牡，非無實之謂也。蔓荊子大，牡荊子小，故呼荊。時珍曰︰古者刑杖以荊，故字從刑。其生成叢而疏爽，故又謂之楚（從林，從匹，匹即疏字也），濟楚之義取此。荊楚之地，因多產此而名也。

【集解】《別錄》曰︰牡荊實生河間、南陽、冤句山谷，或平壽、都鄉高岸上及田野中。

八月、九月採實，陰乾。弘景曰︰論蔓荊即應是今作杖棰之荊。其子殊細，正如小麻子，色青黃。牡荊乃出北方，如烏豆大，正圓黑。仙術多用牡荊，今人都無識者。李當之《藥錄》言︰溲疏一名楊櫨，一名牡荊，理白中虛，斷植即生。按︰今溲疏主療與牡荊都不同，形類乖異。而仙方用牡荊，云能通神見鬼，非惟其實，枝葉並好。又云︰荊樹必枝葉相對者是牡荊，不對者即非牡荊也。並莫詳虛實，更須博訪。恭曰︰牡荊即作棰杖者，所在皆有之。實細黃色，莖勁作樹生。《漢書‧郊祀志》以牡荊莖為幡竿，則明知非蔓荊也。有青、赤二種，以青者為佳。今人相承多以牡荊為蔓荊，此極誤也。頌曰︰牡荊，今眉州、蜀州及近

【釋名】恭曰︰蔓荊苗蔓生，故名。

【集解】恭曰︰蔓荊生水濱。苗莖蔓延長丈餘。春因舊枝而生小葉，五月葉成，似杏葉。六月有花，紅白色，黃蕊。九月有實，黑斑，大如梧子而虛輕。冬則葉凋。今人誤以小荊為蔓荊，遂將蔓荊為牡荊也。

大明曰︰海鹽亦有之。大如豌豆，蒂有輕軟小蓋子，六、七、八月採之。

頌曰︰近汴京及秦、隴、明、越州多有之。苗莖高四、五尺，對節生枝。葉類小楝，至夏盛茂。有花作穗淡紅色，蕊黃白色，花下有青萼，至秋結子。舊說蔓生，而今所有並非蔓也。

宗奭曰︰諸家所解，蔓荊、牡荊，紛紜不一。《經》既言蔓荊明是蔓生，即非高木也；既言牡荊，則自木上生，又何疑焉？

時珍曰︰其枝小弱如蔓，故曰蔓生。

實

【氣味】苦，微寒，無毒。《別錄》曰︰辛，平、溫。

元素曰︰味辛，溫，氣清，陽中之陰，入太陽經。胃虛人不可服，恐生痰疾。

之才曰︰惡烏頭、石膏。

【主治】筋骨間寒熱，濕痹拘攣，明目堅齒，利九竅，去白蟲。久服，輕身耐老。小荊實亦等（《本經》）。風頭痛，腦鳴，目淚出，益氣。令人光澤脂致（《別錄》）。治賊風，長髭發（甄權）。利關節，治癇疾、赤眼（大明）。太陽頭痛，頭沉昏悶，除目暗，散風邪，涼諸經血，止目睛內痛（元素）。搜肝風（好古）。

【發明】恭曰︰小荊實即牡荊子，其功與蔓荊同，故曰亦等也。

時珍曰︰蔓荊氣清味辛，體輕而浮，上行而散。故所主者，皆頭面風虛之症。

**Explanation of Names**
Su Gong [Tang-dynasty commentator] states: The seedlings and stems of Manjing [蔓荊] grow in a trailing, vine-like manner, hence the name [蔓, 'trailing/creeping'].

**Collected Explanations**
Su Gong states: Manjing grows along the banks of waterways. Its stems and shoots trail and extend to more than a zhang [c. 3 metres] in length. In spring, small leaves sprout from the old branches; by the fifth month the leaves are fully formed, resembling apricot leaves. In the sixth month flowers appear, red-white in colour with yellow stamens. In the ninth month it bears fruit — dark-spotted, about the size of a firmiana [Chinese parasol tree, Firmiana simplex] seed, but hollow and light. In winter the leaves fall. People today mistakenly take Xiaojing [小荊, small vitex] for Manjing, and consequently identify Manjing as Mujing [牡荊].

Da Ming [Ri Huazi] states: It is also found in Haiyan [coastal salt regions]. The fruit is about the size of a pea; the stem-end bears a small, light, soft calyx-cap. It is collected in the sixth, seventh, and eighth months.

Su Song [Song-dynasty commentator] states: It is found in abundance near Bianjing [Kaifeng] and in the regions of Qin, Long, Ming, and Yuezhou. The stems grow four to five chi [c. 1.2–1.5 m] tall, with branches growing opposite at each node. The leaves resemble those of the small chinaberry [小楝]; in summer the plant grows lush and dense. The flowers form spikes of pale red, with yellowish-white stamens; below the flowers are green calyces. Fruit forms in autumn. Earlier accounts described it as a creeper, but the plants found today are not creeping at all.

Kou Zongshi [Song-dynasty commentator] states: The various authorities' explanations of Manjing and Mujing are confused and contradictory. The Classics, having clearly stated 'Manjing,' evidently mean a creeping plant — not a tall tree. Having stated 'Mujing,' they mean something that grows as a woody shrub — what doubt can there be?

Li Shizhen states: Its branches are slender and weak like vine tendrils, hence it is called 'creeping' [蔓生].

**Fruit**

**Qi and Flavour**
Bitter, slightly cold, non-toxic. The *Mingyi Bielu* states: acrid [pungent], balanced/neutral, warm.

Zhang Yuansu [Jin-dynasty physician] states: Flavour acrid, warm; its qi is clear. It belongs to the category of yin within yang, and enters the Taiyang channel [Greater Yang meridian]. Persons with stomach deficiency should not take it, for fear of engendering phlegm disorders.

Xu Zhicai states: It is incompatible with [i.e., antagonises] Wutou [aconite root, Aconitum spp.] and Shigao [gypsum, CaSO₄·2H₂O].

**Indications and Treatment**
- Cold and heat [alternating fever and chills] in the sinews and bones; damp impediment [湿痹] with cramping and contracture; brightening the eyes; strengthening the teeth; benefiting the nine orifices; expelling white worms [intestinal parasites]. Long-term use lightens the body and retards aging. The fruit of Xiaojing [小荊] is equivalent in efficacy (*Shennong Bencao Jing*).
- Wind headache; ringing in the brain [tinnitus]; tearing of the eyes; augmenting qi; giving the skin a lustrous, smooth, and well-nourished appearance (*Mingyi Bielu*).
- Treats thieving wind [贼风, sudden pathogenic wind invasion]; promotes growth of the beard and hair (Zhen Quan).
- Benefits the joints; treats epileptic conditions and red/inflamed eyes (Da Ming).
- Taiyang [Greater Yang] headache; heaviness and dull oppression of the head; dispelling dimness of vision; scattering wind pathogen; cooling the blood in all channels; stopping pain within the eyeball (Zhang Yuansu).
- Searches out and expels liver wind [肝风] (Wang Haogu).

**Elucidation**
Su Gong states: The fruit of Xiaojing is precisely the seed of Mujing [牡荊子]; its efficacy is the same as that of Manjing — hence the statement 'equivalent in efficacy.'

Li Shizhen states: Manjing's qi is clear and its flavour acrid; its substance is light and floating. It moves upward and disperses. Therefore all the conditions it governs are wind-deficiency disorders of the head and face.

entities_mentioned.name: 蔓荊 (Manjing)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vitex trifolia L. (three-leaf chaste tree) or Vitex rotundifolia L.f. (beach vitex / roundleaf chaste tree)
entities_mentioned.notes: Both species are used medicinally in traditional Chinese medicine under this name. The trailing, coastal habit described better fits V. rotundifolia, while the taller shrubby descriptions suggest V. trifolia. The dried ripe fruit, known as Manjingzi (蔓荊子), remains an official drug in the Chinese Pharmacopoeia.

entities_mentioned.name: 小荊 / 小荊實 (Xiaojing / Xiaojing fruit)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vitex negundo var. cannabifolia or Vitex negundo (hemp-leaved chaste tree, small vitex)
entities_mentioned.notes: Equated here with Mujing (牡荊); Li Shizhen and Su Gong consider the fruits of Xiaojing and Manjing therapeutically equivalent.

entities_mentioned.name: 牡荊 (Mujing)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vitex negundo L. var. cannabifolia (Siebold & Zucc.) Hand.-Mazz.
entities_mentioned.notes: A woody shrub vitex; conflated with Manjing by some earlier authors, a confusion Li Shizhen seeks to clarify.

entities_mentioned.name: 烏頭 (Wutou)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aconitum carmichaelii Debeaux (aconite / monkshood root)
entities_mentioned.notes: Listed as an incompatible substance (药忌/相恶) with Manjing fruit.

entities_mentioned.name: 石膏 (Shigao)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Gypsum (calcium sulfate dihydrate, CaSO₄·2H₂O)
entities_mentioned.notes: Listed as an incompatible substance with Manjing fruit.

results.translator_notes: 1. The character 紛 in '紛 不一' in the source appears to be a truncated OCR rendering of 紛紜 (fēnyún, 'confused and contradictory'); restored silently. 2. The term '賊風' (thieving wind) refers in classical Chinese medicine to sudden, covert invasion by pathogenic wind — often associated with sudden paralysis or facial palsy. 3. '九竅' (nine orifices) refers to the two eyes, two ears, two nostrils, mouth, urethra, and anus. 4. '太陽經' is the Taiyang meridian (Greater Yang channel), associated in TCM theory with the back of the head and neck; hence the drug's application to occipital/posterior headaches. 5. The Taiyang channel indication and the 'yin within yang' classification are part of Zhang Yuansu's (張元素) systematic drug-channel (引經) theory.

results.title_inferred: Luanjing (Wan Jing / Vitex-like shrub), Wood Section Three
results.original_text:
【釋名】頑荊（《圖經》）。

【集解】恭曰︰欒荊莖、葉都似石南，幹亦反卷，經冬不死，葉上有細黑點者，真也，今雍州所用者是。而洛州乃用石荊當之，非也。俗方大用，而本草不載，亦無別名。但有欒華，功用又別，非此物花也。

頌曰︰欒荊今生東海及淄州、汾州。所生者皆枝莖白，葉小圓而青色，頗似榆葉而長，冬夏不凋。六月開花，花有紫、白二種。子似大麻。四月採苗葉，八月採子。

宗奭曰︰欒荊即牡荊也，子青色如茱萸，不合更立此條。蘇恭又稱石荊當之，轉見穿鑿。

時珍曰︰按︰許慎《說文》云︰欒，似木蘭。木蘭葉似桂，與蘇恭所說葉似石南者相近。蘇頌所圖者即今牡荊，與《唐本草》者不合。欒荊是蘇恭收入本草，不應自誤。蓋後人不識。

子

【氣味】辛、苦，溫，有小毒。

權曰︰甘、辛，微熱，無毒。決明為之使。惡石膏。

【主治】大風，頭面手足諸風，癲癇狂痙，濕痺寒冷疼痛（《唐本》）。四肢不遂，通血脈，明目，益精光（甄權）。

合油同熬，塗人畜瘡疥。
results.translation:
**Explanatory Names:** Wanjing [Stubborn/Intractable Jing] (from the *Illustrated Classic* [Tujing]).

**Collected Commentaries:**

[Su] Gong states: The stems and leaves of Luanjing closely resemble those of Shinan [Photinia / Chinese Photinia]. The trunk also curves backward; it does not die in winter. Those specimens whose leaves bear fine black dots are the genuine article — these are the ones currently used in Yongzhou. In Luozhou, people substitute Shijing [Stone Jing] for it, which is incorrect. It is widely used in common prescriptions, yet is not recorded in the materia medica canon, nor does it have an alternate name. There is a separate entry for Luanhua [Luan flower], whose properties and uses are different — it is not the flower of this plant.

[Su] Song states: Luanjing today grows in the Eastern Sea region and in Zizhou and Fenzhou. The plants there all have white branches and stems; the leaves are small, round, and green, rather resembling elm leaves but more elongated. They do not wither in summer or winter. Flowers appear in the sixth month, coming in two varieties: purple and white. The seeds resemble those of hemp [da ma]. Seedling-leaves are gathered in the fourth month; seeds in the eighth month.

[Kou] Zongshi states: Luanjing is in fact Mujing [Vitex negundo]. Its seeds are green, like those of Zhuyu [Evodia]. It is therefore unnecessary to establish a separate entry for it. Su Gong's further claim that Shijing can be substituted for it only makes things more convoluted and forced.

Li Shizhen states: According to Xu Shen's *Shuowen* [Explaining Writing]: "Luan resembles Mulan [Magnolia]." The leaves of Mulan resemble those of Gui [cinnamon/laurel], which is consistent with what Su Gong says about the leaves resembling Shinan. What Su Song illustrated is the Mujing of today, which does not accord with the *Tang Materia Medica*. Since Luanjing was included in the materia medica by Su Gong himself, he would not have made an error in identifying it. It is likely that later scholars simply could not identify it [and confused the entries].

**Seeds**

**Flavor and Nature:** Acrid [xin], bitter [ku], warm, slightly toxic.

[Zhen] Quan states: Sweet [gan], acrid [xin], mildly hot, non-toxic. Juemingzi [Semen Cassiae] acts as its guide [shi, facilitating herb]. It is antagonistic to Shigao [Gypsum fibrosum].

**Primary Therapeutic Uses:** Great wind [da feng, severe wind-type disorders], various wind conditions affecting the head, face, hands, and feet, epilepsy [dian xian], mania, spasms [kuang jing], damp-impediment [shi bi] with cold pain (*Tang Materia Medica*). Paralysis of the four limbs, promoting circulation in the blood vessels, brightening the eyes, and enhancing the essence of vision (Zhen Quan).

Combined with oil and simmered together, the preparation is applied topically to treat sores and scabies [chuang jie] in both humans and livestock.

entities_mentioned.name: 欒荊 (Luanjing)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Uncertain; possibly Vitex negundo var. or a related Vitex species; confused in historical sources with Mujing (Vitex negundo)
entities_mentioned.notes: Significant historical disagreement among commentators. Kou Zongshi equates it with Mujing (Vitex negundo); Li Shizhen doubts this equivalence but cannot resolve it. Su Gong's original description (leaves like Shinan/Photinia, with black dots) does not clearly match Vitex negundo.

entities_mentioned.name: 石南 (Shinan)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Photinia serrulata (Chinese Photinia) or Rhododendron dauricum; Shinan in classical texts often refers to Photinia
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a morphological reference for leaf comparison.

entities_mentioned.name: 石荊 (Shijing)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Unknown; possibly a local name for a Vitex or Callicarpa species
entities_mentioned.notes: Reportedly used as a substitute for Luanjing in Luozhou, considered incorrect by Su Gong.

entities_mentioned.name: 欒華 (Luanhua)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Koelreuteria paniculata (Golden Rain Tree) flowers
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned to clarify it is a distinct entry with different properties and is not the flower of Luanjing.

entities_mentioned.name: 牡荊 (Mujing)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vitex negundo L. var. cannabifolia or Vitex negundo
entities_mentioned.notes: Kou Zongshi asserts Luanjing is the same as Mujing; Li Shizhen disputes this.

entities_mentioned.name: 木蘭 (Mulan)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Magnolia liliiflora or related Magnolia species
entities_mentioned.notes: Cited from Xu Shen's Shuowen as a morphological analog to Luan.

entities_mentioned.name: 決明 (Juemingzi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Semen Cassiae (seeds of Cassia obtusifolia or Cassia tora)
entities_mentioned.notes: Named as the guide herb (shi) for Luanjing seeds.

entities_mentioned.name: 石膏 (Shigao)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Gypsum fibrosum (hydrated calcium sulfate)
entities_mentioned.notes: Listed as antagonistic (wu) to Luanjing seeds.

entities_mentioned.name: 茱萸 (Zhuyu)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Evodia rutaecarpa (Wu Zhuyu) or Cornus officinalis (Shan Zhuyu)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a size/color comparator for Luanjing seeds.

results.translator_notes:
1. The entry is taxonomically unresolved even within the Bencao Gangmu itself — Li Shizhen acknowledges the confusion without resolving it, which is unusual and worth flagging for botanical researchers.
2. 'Da feng' (大風) in classical Chinese medicine refers to severe wind-type disorders and can encompass conditions such as leprosy (Hansen's disease) as well as stroke-like paralytic conditions — context here suggests a broad neurological/dermatological category.
3. The final line on topical use for sores and scabies in humans and livestock appears to be a brief appended note rather than a formal sub-section.
4. The segment ends mid-entry (after the seeds section); leaves, bark, or other plant part entries may follow in the original but are not present in this segment.
5. OCR/source text ends abruptly with '蓋後人不識' — it is possible the sentence continues in the original ('they could not identify it [and therefore confused the entries]'); the translation renders the most plausible completion.

results.title_inferred: Stone Jing (Shi Jing) — a Water-Side Shrub for Hair Growth
results.original_text:
【集解】藏器曰︰石荊似荊而小，生水旁，《廣濟方》一名水荊是也。蘇頌言洛人以當欒荊者，非也。

【主治】燒灰淋汁浴頭，生發令長（藏器）。
results.translation:
【Collected Explanations】 Chen Cangqi [Zangqi] states: Shijing [Stone Jing] resembles jing [Vitex] but is smaller, and grows beside water. It is the same plant called 'Shuijing' [Water Jing] in the Guangji Fang [Guangji Formulary]. Su Song's claim that people in Luoyang use it as a substitute for Luanjing is incorrect.

【Indications and Treatments】 Burn it to ash, leach the ash with water to produce a lye, and use this liquid to wash the head; it promotes hair growth and lengthens the hair. (Chen Cangqi)

entities_mentioned.name: 石荊 (Shijing / Stone Jing)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a small-leaved Vitex species or a related Lamiaceae shrub growing near water; precise identification uncertain
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as resembling Vitex (荊) but smaller and riparian in habit. Also called 水荊 (Shuijing / Water Jing) in the Guangji Fang. Chen Cangqi explicitly rejects Su Song's equation of this plant with 欒荊 (Luanjing).

entities_mentioned.name: 欒荊 (Luanjing)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vitex negundo or a related Vitex sp.; identity debated in classical sources
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned only to be distinguished from Shijing; Su Song reportedly claimed Luoyang residents used Shijing as a substitute for Luanjing, which Chen Cangqi disputes.

results.translator_notes: The text records a botanical disagreement between Chen Cangqi (陳藏器, Tang dynasty, author of Bencao Shiyi) and Su Song (蘇頌, Song dynasty). The medicinal use — burning to ash, leaching to lye, and washing hair — is a classical alkaline hair treatment; the lye (淋汁) acts as a mild caustic that may stimulate the scalp. The Guangji Fang (廣濟方) is an early Tang imperial formulary. No OCR issues detected.

results.title_inferred: Zǐjīng (Redbud / Chinese Judas Tree) — Wood Section Three
results.original_text:
【釋名】紫珠（《拾遺》），皮名肉紅（《綱目》）、內消。時珍曰：其木似黃荊而色紫，故名。其皮色紅而消腫，故瘍科呼為肉紅，又曰內消，與何首烏同名。

【集解】頌曰：紫荊處處有之，人多種於庭院間。木似黃荊，葉小無丫，花深紫可愛。

藏器曰：即田氏之荊也。至秋子熟，正紫，圓如小珠，名紫珠。江東林澤間尤多。

宗奭曰：春開紫花甚細碎，共作朵生，出無常處，或生於木身之上，或附根上枝下，直出花。花罷葉出，光緊微圓。園圃多植之。

時珍曰：高樹柔條，其花甚繁，歲二、三次。其皮入藥，以川中濃而紫色、味苦如膽者為勝。

木並皮

【氣味】苦，平，無毒。藏器曰：苦，寒。

大明曰：皮、梗及花，氣味功用並同。

【主治】破宿血，下五淋，濃煮汁服（《開寶》）。通小腸（大明）。解諸毒物，癰疽喉痺，飛尸蠱毒，腫下，蛇、虺、蟲、蠶、狂犬毒，並煮汁服。亦以汁洗瘡腫，除血長膚（藏器）。活血行氣，消腫解毒，治婦人血氣疼痛，經水凝澀。

【發明】時珍曰：紫荊氣寒味苦，色紫性降，入手、足厥陰血分。寒勝熱，苦走骨，紫入營。故能活血消腫，利小便而解毒。楊清叟《仙傳方》有沖和膏，以紫荊為君，蓋亦得此意也。其方治一切癰疽發背流注諸腫毒，冷熱不明者。紫荊皮（炒）三兩，獨活（去節，炒）三兩，赤芍藥（炒）二兩，生白芷一兩，木蠟（炒）一兩，為末。用蔥湯調，熱敷。血得熱則行，蔥能散氣也。瘡不甚熱者，酒調之。痛甚者，加乳香。筋不伸者，亦加乳香。大抵癰疽流注，皆是氣血凝滯所成，遇溫則散，遇涼則凝。此方溫平，紫荊皮乃木之精，破血消腫。獨活乃土之精，止風動血，引拔骨中毒，去痺濕氣。芍藥乃火之精，生血止痛。木蠟乃水之精，消腫散血，同獨活能破石腫堅硬。白芷乃金之精，去風生肌止痛。蓋血生則不死，血動則流通，肌生則不爛，痛止則不潰，風去則血自散，氣破則硬可消，毒自除。五者交治，病安有不愈者乎？
results.translation:
**[Explanatory Names]**
Also called Zǐzhū ['Purple Pearl'] (from *Shiyi* [*Materia Medica Supplement*]); the bark is called Ròuhóng ['Flesh-Red'] (from *Bencao Gangmu*) or Nèixiāo ['Internal Disperser']. Li Shizhen states: The tree resembles Huángjīng [Vitex] but is purple in color, hence the name [Zǐjīng, 'Purple Jīng']. Its bark is red in color and disperses swelling, so surgeons [瘍科] call it Ròuhóng, and also Nèixiāo — a name it shares with Héshǒuwū [Polygonum multiflorum].

**[Collected Commentaries]**
Sòng [Su Song] states: Zǐjīng is found everywhere; people commonly plant it in courtyards. The tree resembles Huángjīng, with small leaves that are not forked, and the deep purple flowers are lovely.

Zàngqì [Chen Zangqi] states: This is the jīng tree of the Tián family. In autumn the seeds ripen, perfectly purple and round like small pearls, hence the name Zǐzhū ['Purple Pearl']. It is especially abundant in the forests and wetlands of Jiangdong.

Zōngshì [Kou Zongshi] states: In spring it opens very fine, clustered purple flowers, which emerge in bunches at irregular places — sometimes on the trunk itself, sometimes close to the roots or below the branches, blooming directly from the wood. After the flowers finish, the leaves emerge, which are glossy, firm, and slightly rounded. It is widely cultivated in gardens.

Li Shizhen states: It is a tall tree with pliant branches; the flowers are extremely abundant, blooming two or three times per year. Its bark is used medicinally; that from Sichuan, which is thick, deep purple, and bitter in taste like bile, is considered superior.

**Wood and Bark**

**[Qi and Flavor]**
Bitter, balanced [平], non-toxic. Zàngqì states: Bitter, cold. Dàmíng [Ri Huazi] states: The bark, stems, and flowers are identical in qi, flavor, and medicinal function.

**[Principal Treatments]**
Breaks up stagnant blood [宿血]; relieves the five types of urinary obstruction [五淋] — take a thick decoction of the juice (*Kaibao* [*Kaibao Bencao*]). Promotes flow through the small intestine (Dàmíng). Resolves all manner of toxins: carbuncles and deep abscesses [癰疽], throat obstruction [喉痺], flying-corpse [飛尸] and gu-poison [蠱毒], swelling and redness, bites or stings of snakes, vipers, insects, silkworms, and mad dogs — in all cases decoct and take the juice. The juice may also be used to wash sores and swellings; it removes stagnant blood and promotes growth of new flesh (Zàngqì). Invigorates blood and moves qi, disperses swelling and resolves toxins; treats women's pain due to blood and qi [disorders], and menstrual blockage and congealing.

**[Elucidation]**
Li Shizhen states: Zǐjīng is cold in qi and bitter in flavor; its purple color and descending nature direct it into the blood aspect [血分] of the hand and foot jueyin [pericardium and liver channels]. Cold overcomes heat; bitterness penetrates the bones; purple enters the ying [nutritive level]. Therefore it can invigorate blood, reduce swelling, promote urination, and resolve toxins. Yang Qingsou's *Xianchuanfang* [*Immortal-Transmitted Formulary*] contains the formula Chōnghé Gāo ['Harmonizing Paste'], which uses Zǐjīng as the chief ingredient — a choice that reflects exactly this rationale. That formula treats all carbuncles, back-abscesses [發背], migratory deep abscesses [流注], and various swollen toxins where the distinction between cold and hot is unclear.

*Formula:* Zǐjīng bark (stir-fried) 3 liǎng; Dúhuó [Angelica pubescens] (nodes removed, stir-fried) 3 liǎng; Red peony root [赤芍藥] (stir-fried) 2 liǎng; Fresh Báizhǐ [Angelica dahurica] 1 liǎng; Mùlà [Ligustrum lucidum bark / 'tree wax'] (stir-fried) 1 liǎng. Grind to powder. Mix with scallion [蔥] broth and apply hot as a poultice. Blood moves when warmed, and scallion disperses qi. For sores that are not greatly hot, mix with wine instead. For severe pain, add frankincense [乳香]. For sinews that cannot stretch, also add frankincense.

In general, carbuncles, abscesses, and migratory deep abscesses are all caused by congealing and stagnation of qi and blood — they disperse with warmth and congeal with cold. This formula is warm and balanced. Zǐjīng bark is the essence of Wood [木之精]: it breaks blood and reduces swelling. Dúhuó is the essence of Earth [土之精]: it arrests wind-movement of blood, draws out toxins lodged in the bones, and removes painful obstruction [痺] and damp qi. Peony is the essence of Fire [火之精]: it engenders blood and stops pain. Mùlà is the essence of Water [水之精]: it reduces swelling and disperses blood, and together with Dúhuó can break up hard, stone-like swellings. Báizhǐ is the essence of Metal [金之精]: it expels wind, generates flesh, and stops pain. For when blood is engendered the tissue does not die; when blood moves it flows freely; when flesh is engendered it does not putrefy; when pain is stopped there is no ulceration; when wind is expelled the blood disperses of itself; when qi is broken up, hardness can be dissolved, and toxins are eliminated of themselves. With all five [ingredients] treating [the condition] in concert, how could any illness fail to be cured?

entities_mentioned.name: 紫荊 (Zǐjīng)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cercis chinensis (Chinese redbud / Chinese Judas tree)
entities_mentioned.notes: The bark (皮) is the primary medicinal part. Used to invigorate blood, resolve toxins, reduce swelling, and treat urinary obstruction and women's menstrual disorders. Also called Zǐzhū, Ròuhóng, Nèixiāo.

entities_mentioned.name: 黃荊 (Huángjīng)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vitex negundo
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced as a plant that Zǐjīng resembles in appearance.

entities_mentioned.name: 何首烏 (Héshǒuwū)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Polygonum multiflorum (Reynoutria multiflora)
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned only as sharing the alternative name Nèixiāo.

entities_mentioned.name: 獨活 (Dúhuó)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Angelica pubescens
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in the Chōnghé Gāo formula at 3 liǎng, stir-fried, nodes removed. Described as 'essence of Earth,' treating wind, bone toxins, and damp-obstruction.

entities_mentioned.name: 赤芍藥 (Chì sháoyào)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Paeonia lactiflora or Paeonia veitchii (red peony root)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used at 2 liǎng stir-fried. Described as 'essence of Fire,' engendering blood and stopping pain.

entities_mentioned.name: 白芷 (Báizhǐ)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Angelica dahurica
entities_mentioned.notes: Used fresh at 1 liǎng. Described as 'essence of Metal,' expelling wind, generating flesh, and stopping pain.

entities_mentioned.name: 木蠟 (Mùlà)

results.translator_notes:
1. The character after '腫下' in the main-treatment section appears incomplete in the source (possibly 瘡 or 痢); context suggests 'redness/sores' and has been translated accordingly without addition. Similarly, '痛止則不' at the end of the Elucidation section is followed by a missing character; based on context (antithesis with 'not rotting,' 'not dying') it almost certainly reads 潰 ('ulcerate/rupture'), which has been restored in the cleaned text and translation.
2. 'Five types of urinary obstruction' (五淋) refers to the classical five categories: stone, qi, paste, blood, and labor strangury.
3. The Five Phases (五行) framework is used explicitly to assign each herb a cosmological identity (Wood, Earth, Fire, Water, Metal), which is preserved in the translation.
4. 飛尸 ('flying corpse') and 蠱毒 ('gu toxin') are classical Chinese medical/demonological categories of illness, not literal descriptions; they are rendered with their standard transliterations and brief glosses.
5. The source text assigns the bark's channel entry to 'hand and foot jueyin' — i.e., the pericardium (手厥陰) and liver (足厥陰) channels — consistent with its blood-invigorating actions.

results.title_inferred: Mujin (Rose of Sharon / Hibiscus syriacus) — Bark, Flowers, and Roots
results.original_text:
【釋名】椴（音徒亂切）、櫬（音襯）、蕣（音舜）、日及（《綱目》）、朝開暮落花（《綱目》）、藩籬草（《綱目》）、花奴、王蒸。時珍曰︰此花朝開暮落，故名日及。曰槿曰蕣，猶僅榮一瞬之義也。《爾雅》云︰椴，木槿。櫬，木槿。郭璞注云︰別二名也。或云︰白曰椴，赤曰櫬。齊魯謂之王蒸，言其美而多也。《詩》云「顏如舜華」即此。

【集解】宗奭曰︰木槿花如小葵，淡紅色，五葉成一花，朝開暮斂。湖南北人家多種植為籬障。花與枝兩用。

花小而艷，或白或紅是也。結實輕虛，大葉可茹，作飲代茶。今瘍醫用皮治瘡癬，多取川中來者，濃而色紅。

皮並根

【氣味】甘，平，滑，無毒。大明曰︰涼。
同皮。
同皮。

【主治】止腸風瀉血，痢後熱渴，作飲服之，令人得睡，並炒用（藏器）。治赤白帶下，腫痛疥癬，洗目令明，潤燥活血（時珍）。
腸風瀉血，赤白痢，並焙入藥。作湯代茶，治風（大明）。消瘡腫，利小便，除濕熱（時珍）。
偏正頭風，燒煙熏患處。又治黃水膿瘡，燒存性，豬骨髓調塗之（時珍）。

【發明】時珍曰︰木槿皮及花，並滑如葵花，故能潤燥。色如紫荊，故能活血。川中來者，氣濃力優，故尤有效。
results.translation:
**[Names and Nomenclature]**
Alternate names: Duàn [椴, pronounced tú-luàn-qiē], Chèn [櫬, pronounced chèn], Shùn [蕣, pronounced shùn], Rìjí [日及] (Bencao Gangmu), Zhāo kāi mù luò huā [朝開暮落花, 'Flower That Opens at Dawn and Falls at Dusk'] (Bencao Gangmu), Fánlí cǎo [藩籬草, 'Hedge Grass'] (Bencao Gangmu), Huānú [花奴, 'Flower Servant'], and Wángzhēng [王蒸].

Li Shizhen states: This flower opens in the morning and falls in the evening, hence the name Rìjí ['reaching only one day']. The names Jǐn [槿] and Shùn [蕣] carry the meaning of flourishing for only a single instant. The Erya states: 'Duàn is mùjǐn; Chèn is mùjǐn.' Guo Pu's commentary notes these are simply two different names for the same plant. Some say the white-flowered variety is called Duàn and the red-flowered variety is called Chèn. The people of Qi and Lu call it Wángzhēng, indicating its beauty and abundance. The line in the Book of Odes, 'A complexion like the Shùn flower,' refers to this plant.

**[Collected Explanations]**
Kou Zongshi states: The flower of mùjǐn resembles a small mallow [葵], is pale red in color, has five petals forming a single flower, opens at dawn, and closes at dusk. Families in Hunan and Hubei often plant it as a hedge or fence. Both the flowers and the branches are used medicinally.

The flowers are small but vivid, either white or red. The fruit is light and hollow. The large leaves are edible and can be steeped as a tea substitute. Physicians who treat sores [瘍醫] currently use the bark to treat sores and ringworm [瘡癬]; most prefer specimens from Sichuan, which are thick and reddish.

**Bark and Root**

**[Qi and Flavor]**
Sweet, balanced [平], smooth, non-toxic. Da Ming states: cooling in nature.
[Flowers and leaves share the same properties as the bark.]

**[Indications and Treatments]**
- Stops intestinal wind [腸風] with bloody diarrhea; for thirst and fever following dysentery, prepare as a decoction and drink it — it also promotes sleep. Used stir-fried [炒用]. (Chen Cangqi)
- Treats red and white vaginal discharge [赤白帶下], swelling and pain, scabies and ringworm [疥癬]; used as an eye wash to brighten vision; moistens dryness and invigorates the blood. (Li Shizhen)
- For intestinal wind with bloody stools and red or white dysentery, dry-roast [焙] and incorporate into medicine. Prepared as a tea-substitute decoction, it treats wind disorders. (Da Ming)
- Disperses sores and swelling, promotes urination, eliminates damp-heat. (Li Shizhen)
- For unilateral or bilateral headache [偏正頭風], burn and fumigate the affected area with the smoke. Also treats yellow-water festering sores [黃水膿瘡]: burn to preserve the medicinal properties [燒存性], mix with pig bone marrow, and apply topically. (Li Shizhen)

**[Elucidation]**
Li Shizhen states: The bark and flowers of mùjǐn are both smooth and slippery like mallow flowers, and thus have the ability to moisten dryness. Their color resembles that of redbud [紫荊], and thus they can invigorate the blood. Specimens from Sichuan are dense and potent in qi, and therefore especially effective.

results.translator_notes: 1. The original OCR rendered the character 蕣 (shùn) as a blank space in the 釋名 section and appears to have partially garbled the 集解 section ('或白或是也' is likely '或白或紅是也' — 'either white or red'; this has been silently corrected in the cleaned text). 2. The phrase 燒存性 ('burn to preserve the medicinal properties') is a standard pharmaceutical preparation term in classical Chinese medicine, referring to charring a substance while preserving its inherent potency, roughly analogous to 'calcination' retaining active constituents. 3. 腸風 ('intestinal wind') is a classical TCM diagnostic category generally corresponding to hemorrhagic bowel conditions such as hematochezia. 4. The line from the Book of Odes (詩經) '顏如舜華' comes from the 'Zheng Feng' section ('有女同車') and is a famous simile comparing a woman's complexion to the shùn flower.

results.title_inferred: Fusang (Hibiscus rosa-sinensis) — Leaves and Flowers
results.original_text:
【釋名】佛桑（《霏雪錄》）、朱槿（《草木狀》）

時珍曰：東海日出處有扶桑樹。此花光艷照日，其葉似桑，因以比之。後人訛為佛桑，乃木槿別種，故日及諸名亦與之同。

【集解】時珍曰：扶桑產南方，乃木槿別種。其枝柯柔弱，葉深綠，微澀如桑。其花有紅、黃、白三色，紅者尤貴，呼為朱槿。嵇含《草木狀》云：朱槿一名赤槿，一名日及，出高涼郡。花、莖、葉皆如桑。其葉光而濃。木高四、五尺，而枝葉婆娑。其花深紅色，五出，大如蜀葵，重敷柔澤。有蕊一條，長於花葉，上綴金屑，日光所爍，疑若焰生。一叢之上，日開數百朵，朝開暮落。自二月始，至中冬乃歇。插枝即活。

葉及花

【氣味】甘，平，無毒。

【主治】癰疽腮腫，取葉或花，同白芙蓉葉、牛蒡葉、白蜜研膏敷之，即散（時珍）
results.translation:
**Names and Nomenclature**
Alternate names: Fosang [Buddha-mulberry] (from *Feixue Lu*); Zhujin [Crimson Hibiscus] (from *Caomuzhuang*).

Li Shizhen states: In the eastern sea, where the sun rises, there is said to grow a great tree called Fusang. This flower is brilliantly radiant in sunlight, and its leaves resemble those of the mulberry, hence the comparison. Later people corrupted the name to 'Fosang' [Buddha-mulberry]. It is a distinct variety of Mujin [Hibiscus syriacus], and accordingly shares several of the same alternate names, including 'Riji' [daily renewal].

**Collected Explanations**
Li Shizhen states: Fusang is produced in the southern regions and is a distinct variety of Mujin. Its branches are slender and pliable, its leaves are deep green and slightly rough, resembling mulberry leaves. Its flowers come in three colors — red, yellow, and white — of which red is most prized, and is called Zhujin [Crimson Hibiscus]. Ji Han's *Caomuzhuang* [Records of Plants and Trees] states: Zhujin is also known as Chijin [Red Hibiscus] and Riji [Daily-opening]; it originates from Gaoliang Commandery. The flowers, stems, and leaves all resemble those of the mulberry. The leaves are glossy and thick. The tree grows four to five feet tall, with branches and foliage spreading gracefully. Its flowers are deep red, with five petals, as large as those of the Shu mallow [Alcea rosea], richly layered and softly lustrous. There is a single stamen column, longer than the petals, adorned at the tip with golden specks that glitter in sunlight as though flames were dancing. A single clump may open several hundred blossoms in a day; they open in the morning and fall by evening. Flowering begins in the second month [approximately March] and does not cease until mid-winter. It propagates readily from cuttings.

**Leaves and Flowers**

**Flavor and Nature:** Sweet; balanced [neutral]; non-toxic.

**Indications and Treatment:** For carbuncles, abscesses, and swelling of the cheeks [parotid region]: take the leaves or flowers together with white Furong [Confederate rose, *Hibiscus mutabilis*] leaves, burdock [*Arctium lappa*] leaves, and white honey; grind into a paste and apply externally — the swelling will disperse. (Li Shizhen)

entities_mentioned.name: 扶桑 / 佛桑 / 朱槿 / 赤槿 / 日及

entities_mentioned.notes: Multiple alternate names are recorded: Fusang, Fosang, Zhujin (Crimson Hibiscus), Chijin (Red Hibiscus), Riji (Daily-opening). Li Shizhen distinguishes it as a southern variety related to but distinct from Mujin (Hibiscus syriacus). Description of five-petaled deep red flowers with prominent stamen column strongly matches H. rosa-sinensis.

results.translator_notes: The source text is cut off mid-sentence at the end ('即散（時珍') — the closing parenthesis and possibly further text following the attribution to Li Shizhen appear to be missing from the supplied segment. The medical entry has been translated as far as the text permits. The mythological Fusang tree of Chinese cosmology (a giant tree at the sunrise point in the eastern sea) is invoked etymologically but is not the same plant; Li Shizhen explicitly acknowledges the metaphorical naming. 'Gaoliang Commandery' (高涼郡) was a historical administrative region in what is now coastal Guangdong province, consistent with the southern range of H. rosa-sinensis.

results.title_inferred: Mù Fúróng (Confederate Rose / Cotton Rose): Names, Description, and Medicinal Uses
results.original_text:
【釋名】地芙蓉（《圖經》）、木蓮（《綱目》）

時珍曰︰此花艷如荷花，故有芙蓉、木蓮之名。八、九月始開，故名拒霜。俗呼為芙皮樹。《相如賦》謂之華木。注云︰皮可為索也。蘇東坡詩云︰喚作拒霜猶未稱，看來卻是最宜霜。蘇頌《圖經本草》有地芙蓉，云出鼎州，九月採葉，治瘡。

【集解】時珍曰；木芙蓉處處有之，插條即生，小木也。其幹叢生如荊，高者丈許。其葉大如桐，有五尖及七尖者，冬凋夏茂。秋半始著花，花類牡丹、芍藥，有紅者、白者、黃者、千葉者，最耐寒而不落。不結實。山人取其皮為索。川、廣有添色拒霜花，初開白色，次日稍紅，又明日則深紅，先後相間如數色。霜時采花，霜後採葉，陰乾入藥。

葉並花

【氣味】微辛，平，無毒。

【主治】清肺涼血，散熱解毒，治一切大小癰疽腫毒惡瘡，消腫排膿止痛（時珍）。

【發明】時珍曰︰芙蓉花並葉，氣平而不寒不熱，味微辛而性滑涎粘，其治癰腫之功，殊有神效。近時瘍醫秘其名為清涼膏、清露散、鐵箍散，皆此物也。其方治一切癰疽發背，乳癰惡瘡，不拘已成未成，已穿未穿。並用芙蓉葉，或根皮，或花，或生研，或乾研末，以蜜調塗於腫處四圍，中間留頭，乾則頻換。初起者，即覺清涼，痛止腫消。已成者，即膿聚毒出。已穿者，即膿出易斂。妙不可言。或加生赤小豆末，尤妙。
results.translation:
**[Names]** Also called Dì Fúróng [Ground Lotus] (according to the *Illustrated Classic* [Tújīng]); Mù Lián [Woody Lotus] (according to the *Compendium* [Gāngmù]).

Li Shizhen states: The flower is as brilliantly beautiful as the lotus blossom [hé huā], hence the names Fúróng [Lotus] and Mù Lián [Woody Lotus]. It begins to bloom in the eighth and ninth months, and is therefore called Jù Shuāng [Frost-Defying]. Commonly it is called the Fúpí Tree [Bast Tree]. In the Rhapsody of Sima Xiangru it is referred to as Huá Mù [Splendid Wood]; the commentary notes that its bark can be used to make rope. Su Dongpo's poem reads: 'To call it Frost-Defying is still not quite right — looking at it, it clearly suits the frost best.' Su Song's *Illustrated Classic of Materia Medica* [Tújīng Běncǎo] includes an entry for Dì Fúróng, stating it comes from Dingzhou, that its leaves are gathered in the ninth month, and that it is used to treat sores.

**[Collected Explanations]** Li Shizhen states: Mù Fúróng grows everywhere; it readily propagates from cuttings, and is a small tree. Its trunk grows in dense clumps like the vitex shrub, and the tallest specimens reach about one zhàng [approx. 3 meters] in height. Its leaves are as large as those of the paulownia tree, with five or seven pointed lobes; they wither in winter and flourish in summer. It begins to flower in mid-autumn; the blossoms resemble those of the tree peony [mǔdān] and herbaceous peony [sháoyào], and occur in red, white, yellow, and double-petaled [qiānyè] forms. The flowers are exceptionally frost-hardy and do not fall. The plant bears no fruit. Mountain-dwelling people use the bark to make rope. In Sichuan and Guangdong there is a color-changing Jù Shuāng flower: when it first opens it is white, by the following day it is somewhat red, and by the day after that it is deep red, so that at any one time flowers of several colors appear together on the plant. Flowers are gathered at the time of frost; leaves are gathered after the frost. Both are shade-dried before use in medicine.

**Leaves and Flowers**

**[Qi and Flavor]** Slightly acrid [xīn], balanced [píng], non-toxic.

**[Indications]** Clears the lungs and cools the blood; disperses heat and resolves toxins; treats all manner of large and small abscesses [yōng] and swellings [jū], toxic swellings, and malignant sores; reduces swelling, drains pus, and relieves pain. (Li Shizhen)

**[Elaboration]** Li Shizhen states: The flowers and leaves of Mù Fúróng have a balanced qi — neither cold nor hot; the flavor is mildly acrid and the nature is slippery, mucilaginous, and adhesive. Their efficacy in treating abscesses and swellings is truly remarkable. Contemporary physicians specializing in sores [yáng yī] have given it secret proprietary names such as Qīngliáng Gāo [Cool and Refreshing Plaster], Qīnglù Sǎn [Clear Dew Powder], and Tiějū Sǎn [Iron-Binding Powder] — all of these are simply this same drug. The formula treats all abscesses [yōng] and swellings [jū] of the back, breast abscesses [rǔ yōng], and malignant sores, regardless of whether they have yet formed or already formed, already ulcerated or not yet ulcerated. One uses Fúróng leaves, or the bark of the root, or the flowers — either freshly ground or dried and powdered — mixed with honey and applied in a ring around the swelling, leaving an opening at the head; when it dries, it should be renewed frequently. At the onset [of a lesion], the patient immediately feels a cooling sensation; pain ceases and the swelling subsides. When the lesion has already formed, the pus is gathered and the toxin expelled. When the lesion has already ulcerated, the pus drains and the wound closes easily. The effects are beyond description. Adding freshly ground red adzuki bean [chì xiǎodòu] powder is even more effective.

entities_mentioned.name: 木芙蓉 (Mù Fúróng)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Hibiscus mutabilis L. (Confederate rose / Cotton rose)
entities_mentioned.notes: Also recorded under the synonyms 地芙蓉 (Dì Fúróng), 木蓮 (Mù Lián), and 拒霜 (Jù Shuāng). The text notes a color-changing variety from Sichuan and Guangdong, consistent with H. mutabilis 'Versicolor'. Used medicinally as leaves, flowers, and root bark.

entities_mentioned.name: 赤小豆 (Chì Xiǎodòu)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vigna umbellata (Rice bean) or Vigna angularis (Adzuki bean)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here in powdered form as an adjuvant added to the topical paste preparation. Classical Chinese texts sometimes conflate these two Vigna species under this name.

entities_mentioned.name: 清涼膏 (Qīngliáng Gāo)
entities_mentioned.notes: A proprietary name used by contemporary wound physicians (瘍醫) for preparations based on Hibiscus mutabilis.

entities_mentioned.name: 清露散 (Qīnglù Sǎn)
entities_mentioned.notes: Another proprietary name for the same Hibiscus mutabilis preparation.

entities_mentioned.name: 鐵箍散 (Tiějū Sǎn)
entities_mentioned.notes: A third proprietary name for the same Hibiscus mutabilis preparation. The name 'Iron-Binding Powder' evokes the ability of the paste to encircle and contain the lesion.

results.translator_notes:
1. The original text as received contains a probable OCR dropout in '俗呼為皮樹' — most editions read '芙皮樹' or '拒霜樹' in full; the cleaned text has been amended to '芙皮樹' as the most plausible reading, though some editions simply read '皮樹' as a standalone colloquial name referring to the fibrous bark.
2. The bracketed attribution '（時珍）' at the end of the [Indications] section was truncated in the source (likely cut off from a longer attribution) and has been silently completed.
3. 'Jù' (疽) and 'Yōng' (癰) are distinct classical terms: yōng typically denotes superficial, hot abscesses, while jū refers to deeper, often more serious carbuncles. Both are rendered with descriptive English equivalents and the romanized terms retained.
4. Su Song's 《圖經本草》 (Illustrated Classic of Materia Medica, 1061 CE) is cited as a source for the Dingzhou provenance of Dì Fúróng.
5. The Sima Xiangru reference is to the 《上林賦》 (Rhapsody on the Imperial Hunting Park), a Han dynasty literary text.

results.title_inferred: Mountain Camellia (Shancha) — Flower and Seed Uses
results.original_text:
【釋名】時珍曰︰其葉類茗，又可作飲，故得茶名。

【集解】時珍曰︰山茶產南方。樹生，高者丈許，枝幹交加。葉頗似茶葉，而濃硬有棱，中闊頭尖，面綠背淡。深冬開花，紅瓣黃蕊。《格古論》云︰花有數種︰寶珠者，花簇如珠，最勝。海榴茶花蒂青，石榴茶中有碎花，躑躅茶花如杜鵑花，宮粉茶、串珠茶皆粉紅色。又有一捻紅、千葉紅、千葉白等名，不可勝數，葉各小異。或云亦有黃色者。《虞衡志》廣中有南山茶，花大倍中州者，色微淡，葉薄有毛。結實如梨，大如拳，中有數核，如肥皂子大。周定王《救荒本草》云︰山茶嫩葉炸熟水淘可食，亦可蒸晒作飲。

花

【氣味】缺。

**Collected Commentaries:** Shizhen states: Mountain camellia [山茶] is produced in the southern regions. It grows as a tree, reaching a height of up to one zhang [approx. 3 metres] for the tallest specimens, with branches and trunk intertwining. The leaves closely resemble tea leaves but are thicker, stiffer, and ridged, broad in the middle and tapering to a point at the tip, green on the upper surface and pale beneath. Flowers open in the depths of winter, with red petals and yellow stamens. The 'Gegu Lun' [格古論, 'Discourse on the Investigation of Antiquities'] states: there are several varieties of flower. The 'Hailiucha' [Sea-Pomegranate Camellia] has a greenish calyx; the 'Shiliucha' [Pomegranate Camellia] bears small, clustered petals within; the 'Zhizhicha' [Rhododendron Camellia] has flowers resembling azalea blooms; the 'Gongfencha' [Palace Pink Camellia] and 'Chuanzhicha' [Strung-Pearl Camellia] are both pink. Some report that yellow-flowered specimens also exist. The 'Yuhengzhi' [虞衡志] records that in the Guang [Guangdong/Guangxi] region there is a 'Southern Mountain Camellia' whose flowers are twice the size of those found in the central provinces, with a slightly paler colour; the leaves are thin and covered in fine hairs. It bears fruit resembling a pear, as large as a fist, containing several seeds inside, each approximately the size of a gleditsia seed [皁莢子]. Prince Zhouding's 'Jiuhuang Bencao' [救荒本草, 'Famine Relief Materia Medica'] states: the tender leaves of mountain camellia can be deep-fried, then rinsed in water and eaten; they may also be steamed and sun-dried to make a beverage.

【釋名】黃梅花。

時珍曰︰此物本非梅類，因其與梅同時，香又相近，色似蜜蠟，故得此名。
