results.translation:
**[Names]** Yellow Plum Blossom [Huángméi Huā].

Li Shizhen states: This plant is not truly a member of the plum [méi] family, but because it blooms at the same time as the plum, its fragrance is similar, and its color resembles beeswax [mìlà], it received this name.

**[Collected Explanations]** Li Shizhen states: Làméi is a small tree with clustered branches and pointed leaves. There are altogether three varieties: those grown from seed without grafting bloom with small flowers in the twelfth lunar month [làyuè] and have a light fragrance — these are called Gǒuyíng Méi ["Dog-fly Plum"]; those that have been grafted but bloom sparsely, with flowers that remain half-closed at the mouth when open, are called Qìngkǒu Méi ["Chime-mouth Plum"]; those with dense flowers, a rich fragrance, and a deep yellow color resembling zitan [rosewood/Pterocarpus santalinus], are called Tánxiāng Méi ["Sandalwood-fragrance Plum"] — this last is the finest. The fruit hangs like a drooping bell, pointed and over an inch in length, with seeds inside. The bark of the tree, when soaked in water and used to grind ink, imparts a lustrous sheen.

**Flowers**

**[Flavor and Nature]** Acrid [xīn], warm, non-toxic.

**[Primary Indications]** Relieves summerheat and promotes the generation of fluids [jiěshǔ shēngjīn]. (Li Shizhen)

entities_mentioned.name: 蠟梅 (Làméi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Chimonanthus praecox (wintersweet), family Calycanthaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Li Shizhen explicitly notes it is not a true plum (Prunus mume). Three cultivar types are described: ungrafted seedling type (Gǒuyíng Méi), grafted sparse-flowering type (Qìngkǒu Méi), and the prized dense-flowering, deeply yellow type (Tánxiāng Méi).

entities_mentioned.name: 狗蠅梅 (Gǒuyíng Méi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Seedling-grown Chimonanthus praecox (ungrafted form)
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as having lighter fragrance; grown from seed without grafting.

entities_mentioned.name: 磬口梅 (Qìngkǒu Méi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Grafted Chimonanthus praecox cultivar with semi-closed flowers
entities_mentioned.notes: Named for the half-open, 'chime-mouth' shape of the flower when blooming.

entities_mentioned.name: 檀香梅 (Tánxiāng Méi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Prized cultivar of Chimonanthus praecox with deep yellow, dense, fragrant flowers
entities_mentioned.notes: Considered the best variety; color compared to zitan (Pterocarpus santalinus or related rosewood species).

results.translator_notes: The medical application is minimal: only the flowers are given a therapeutic entry. The indicated use — 'relieves summerheat and generates fluids' — is a standard tonic/cooling function in classical Chinese medicine. The note about using the bark-infused water to grind ink is a practical/craft observation rather than a medicinal one. 'Làyuè' (臘月) refers to the twelfth lunar month, roughly January, which is when wintersweet famously blooms in China. The comparison of flower color to 紫檀 (zǐtán) is somewhat unexpected since zitan wood is typically dark purple-red; Li Shizhen here likely refers to the deep golden-yellow of a particular heartwood grain, or may mean a golden-brown rosewood variety.

results.title_inferred: Fúniú Flower (Buxus-spine flower / Damnacanthus): Uses for Wind-Dampness and Pain
results.original_text:
【釋名】隔虎刺花（未詳）。

【集解】頌曰︰伏牛花生蜀地，所在皆有，今惟益州蜀地有之，似黃柏葉而不光。莖赤有刺。開花淡黃色作穗，似杏花而小，三月採，陰乾。又睦州所生虎刺，云凌冬不凋，彼人無時采根、葉，治風腫疾。

花

【氣味】苦、甘，平，無毒。

【主治】久風濕痺，四肢拘攣，骨肉疼痛。

作湯，治風眩頭痛，五痔下血（《開寶》）。
一切腫痛風疾，細銼焙研，每服一錢匕，用溫酒調下（頌）。

【發明】時珍曰；伏牛花治風濕有名，而用者頗少。楊子建《護命方》有伏牛花散，治男女一切頭風，發作有時，甚則大腑熱秘。用伏牛花、山茵陳、桑寄生、白牽牛、川芎、白僵蠶、蠍梢各二錢，荊芥穗四錢，為末。每服二錢，水煎一沸，連滓服。

根、葉、枝
results.translation:
**[Names]** Also called Géhǔcìhuā [Hedge Tiger-Thorn Flower] (origin of name unclear).

**[Collected Commentaries]** Su Song [Sòng] states: Fúniú flower grows in the Shu region [Sichuan]; it is found in many places, but today it is found only in Yizhou [Chengdu area] in Shu. Its leaves resemble those of Huángbò [Phellodendron] but are not glossy. The stems are red and bear thorns. It produces pale yellow flowers in spikes, resembling apricot blossoms but smaller. It is collected in the third month [March–April] and dried in the shade. There is also a Tiger-Thorn [Hǔcì] growing in Muzhou, said to remain evergreen through winter; the local people collect the roots and leaves at any season to treat wind-swelling ailments.

**Flower**

**[Flavor and Nature]** Bitter, sweet; balanced [ping]; non-toxic.

**[Indications and Treatments]**
Chronic wind-dampness impediment [fēngshī bì], contracture of the four limbs, and aching pain in the bones and flesh.

Prepared as a decoction [tāng], it treats wind-dizziness [fēngxuàn], headache, and rectal bleeding from the five types of hemorrhoids [wǔzhì]. (Source: *Kāibǎo [Bencao]*)

For all swelling pain and wind ailments: finely chop, dry-roast, and grind into powder; take one qiánbǐ [approx. 1 qian, ~3–4 g] per dose, stirred into warm wine. (Source: Su Song)

**[Elucidation]** Li Shizhen states: Fúniú flower is well known for treating wind-dampness, yet it is seldom used in practice. Yang Zijian's *Hùmìng Fāng* [Formulary for Protecting Life] contains a formula called Fúniú Flower Powder [伏牛花散], used to treat all manner of head-wind [tóufēng] in men and women, attacks that occur at irregular intervals, and in severe cases, heat-constipation of the large intestine. The formula uses: Fúniú flower, mountain yinchen [Artemisia capillaris], Sāng jìshēng [Loranthus/mistletoe], white morning glory seeds [Bái qiānniú], Chuān xiōng [Ligusticum chuanxiong], white stiffened silkworm [Bái jiāngcán], and scorpion tail [Xiē shāo], each two qian; Jīngjié spike [Schizonepeta tenuifolia] four qian. Grind to a powder. Take two qian per dose, brought to a single boil in water, and swallowed together with the dregs.

**Roots, Leaves, and Branches** [entry continues — text not provided in this segment]

results.title_inferred: Mìméng Flower (Buddleja officinalis) — Medicinal Uses and Description
results.original_text:
【釋名】水錦花（《炮炙論》）。

時珍曰︰其花繁密蒙茸如簇錦，故名。

【集解】頌曰︰密蒙花，蜀中州郡皆有之。樹高丈餘。葉似冬青葉而濃，背白有細毛，又似橘葉。花微紫色。二月、三月採花，曝乾用。

宗奭曰︰利州甚多。葉冬不凋，亦不似冬青，柔而不光潔，不深綠。其花細碎，數十房成一朵，冬生春開。

花

【氣味】甘，平、微寒，無毒。

【主治】青盲膚翳，赤澀多眵淚，消目中赤脈，小兒麩豆及疳氣攻眼（《開寶》），羞明怕日（劉守真）。入肝經氣、血分，潤肝燥。
results.translation:
**[Explanatory Names]** Water-brocade Flower [水錦花] (from the *Paozhi Lun* [Treatise on Preparation]).

Li Shizhen states: Its blossoms are densely clustered and fluffy, like gathered brocade, hence the name [mìméng, 'dense and lush'].

**[Collected Commentaries]** Su Song states: Mìméng flower is found throughout the prefectures and districts of Shu [modern Sichuan]. The tree grows to more than one zhang [approx. 3+ metres] in height. The leaves resemble those of winter-green [冬青, Ilex] but are thicker, with a white underside covered in fine hairs; they also resemble tangerine leaves. The flowers are a pale purple. Flowers are gathered in the second and third months [of the lunar calendar] and dried in the sun for use.

Kou Zongshi states: The plant is found in great abundance in Lizhou. Its leaves do not wither in winter, yet they do not truly resemble those of winter-green — they are soft rather than glossy and smooth, and not a deep green. The flowers are fine and delicate; tens of florets form a single cluster. They emerge in winter and open in spring.

**Flower**

**[Qi and Flavour]** Sweet; balanced [平], slightly cold; non-toxic.

**[Main Treatments]** Blue-blindness [青盲, amaurosis] and superficial eye-screen [膚翳, corneal opacity]; redness, roughness, and excessive discharge with tearing of the eyes; disperses red veins in the eye; in children: pox-related eye affections [麩豆] and *gan* [疳, nutritional-deficiency syndrome] qi attacking the eyes (from the *Kaibao [Bencao]*); photophobia and sensitivity to sunlight (Liu Shouzhen). [The flower] enters the qi and blood aspects of the Liver channel; moistens Liver dryness.

entities_mentioned.name: 密蒙花 (Mìméng Huā)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Buddleja officinalis Maxim. (Butterfly bush / Pale buddleja); family Scrophulariaceae (or Buddlejaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Also known as 水錦花 (Water-brocade Flower). Widely used in traditional Chinese ophthalmology. The dried flower buds remain a pharmacopoeia-listed herb in modern TCM.

entities_mentioned.name: 冬青 (Dōngqīng)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ilex purpurea or related Ilex spp. (Holly / Winter-green)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a comparative reference for leaf morphology, not as a medicinal substance in this entry.

results.title_inferred: Cotton (Mùmián) — Tree and Herbaceous Forms
results.original_text:
【釋名】古貝（《綱目》）、古終。

時珍曰：木綿有二種：似木者名古貝，似草者名古終。或作吉貝者，乃古貝之訛也。梵書謂之婆，又曰迦羅婆劫。

【集解】時珍曰：木綿有草、木二種。交廣木綿，樹大如抱。其枝似桐。其葉大，如胡桃葉。入秋開花，紅如山茶花，黃蕊，花片極濃，為房甚繁，逼側相比。結實大如拳，實中有白綿，綿中有子。今人謂之斑枝花，訛為攀枝花。李延壽《南史》所謂林邑諸國出古貝花，中如鵝毳，抽其緒，紡為布；張勃《吳錄》所謂交州、永昌木綿樹高過屋，有十餘年不換者，實大如杯，花中綿軟白，可為絮及毛布者，皆指似木之木綿也。江南、淮北所種木綿，四月下種，莖弱如蔓，高者四、五尺，葉有三尖如楓葉，入秋開花黃色，如葵花而小，亦有紅紫者，結實大如桃，中有白綿，綿中有子，大如梧子，亦有紫綿者，八月採，謂之綿花；李延壽《南史》所謂高昌國有草，實如繭，中絲為細，名曰白疊，取以為帛，甚軟白；沈懷遠《南越志》所謂桂州出古終藤，結實如鵝毳，核如珠，治出其核，紡如絲綿，染為斑布者，皆指似草之木綿也。此種出南番，宋末始入江南，今則遍及江北與中州矣。不蠶而綿，不麻而布，利被天下，其益大哉。又《南越志》言：南詔諸蠻不養蠶，惟收娑羅木子中白絮，紉為絲，織為幅，名娑羅籠段。祝穆《方輿志》言：平緬出娑羅樹，大者高三、五丈，結子有綿，紉綿織為白氈兜羅錦。此亦斑枝花之類，各方稱呼不同耳。

白綿及布

【氣味】甘，溫，無毒。
辛，熱，微毒。

【主治】血崩金瘡，燒灰用（時珍）。

子油（用兩瓶合燒取瀝。）
惡瘡疥癬。燃燈，損目（時珍）。
results.translation:
**Names and Nomenclature**
Also known as Gǔbèi [古貝] (Bencao Gangmu) and Gǔzhōng [古終].

Li Shizhen states: There are two kinds of mùmián [cotton/kapok]: the tree-form is called Gǔbèi, and the herb-form is called Gǔzhōng. The variant spelling Jíbèi [吉貝] is a corruption of Gǔbèi. Sanskrit texts call it [pō-pó / bā], and also Jiāluó-pójié [迦羅婆劫].

**Collected Explanations**
Li Shizhen states: There are two kinds of mùmián — the herb-form and the tree-form.

The mùmián of Jiao[zhou] and Guang[zhou] is a tree as large as one can wrap one's arms around. Its branches resemble the paulownia. Its leaves are large, like walnut leaves. In autumn it flowers, red as camellia blossoms, with yellow stamens; the petals are very thick, and the flower clusters are extremely dense, pressed close together. The fruits are as large as a fist; inside the fruit is white floss [bái mián], and within the floss are seeds. People today call it bānzhīhuā [斑枝花], sometimes corrupted to pānzhīhuā [攀枝花]. Li Yanshou's Nanshi [History of the Southern Dynasties] states that the countries of Linyi and others produce the Gǔbèi flower, whose interior is like goose down; one draws out the fibers and spins them into cloth. Zhang Bo's Wulu [Records of Wu] states that in Jiaozhou and Yongchang the mùmián trees grow taller than houses; some go more than ten years without being replaced; the fruits are as large as cups; the floss within the flowers is soft and white, and can be used for wadding and woolen cloth — all of these refer to the tree-form of mùmián.

The mùmián cultivated in Jiangnan and north of the Huai River is sown in the fourth month. The stems are slender and vine-like, growing four to five feet tall at most. The leaves have three points like maple leaves. In autumn it flowers yellow, smaller than a mallow blossom, though red and purple varieties also exist. The fruits are as large as peaches; inside is white floss, and within the floss are seeds as large as parasol-tree seeds [wútóng seeds]; purple-floss varieties also exist. It is harvested in the eighth month and is called miánhuā [綿花, cotton flower]. Li Yanshou's Nanshi states that in the kingdom of Gaochang there is a plant whose fruits resemble cocoons, with fine silk-like fibers inside, called báidié [白疊, white cotton]; cloth made from it is very soft and white. Shen Huaiyuan's Nanyue Zhi [Records of Southern Yue] states that Guizhou produces the Gǔzhōng vine, whose fruits resemble goose down, with pits like beads; after the pits are removed, the fibers are spun like silk-floss and dyed into patterned cloth — all of these refer to the herb-form of mùmián.

This variety originated in the southern barbarian lands and only entered Jiangnan at the end of the Song dynasty; today it has spread throughout Jiangbei and the Central Plains. It produces floss without silkworms, and cloth without hemp — its benefits extend across the world; truly its value is immense.

The Nanyue Zhi also states: The various Man [barbarian] peoples of Nanzhao do not raise silkworms; they collect only the white floss from the seeds of the suōluó tree [娑羅木], twist it into thread, and weave it into cloth, called suōluólóngduàn [娑羅籠段]. Zhu Mu's Fangyu Zhi [Gazetteer of Regions] states: Pingmian [in present-day Burma] produces the suōluó tree; the largest grow thirty to fifty feet tall; the seeds bear floss, which is twisted and woven into white felt and dūluójǐn [兜羅錦, duola brocade]. These too are of the same class as bānzhīhuā, merely called by different names in different regions.

**White Floss and Cloth made from it**

*Flavor and Nature:* Sweet, warm, non-toxic.
[The seed oil is:] Acrid, hot, slightly toxic.

*Primary Therapeutic Uses:*
- Uterine flooding [blood collapse, 血崩] and sword/metal wounds [金瘡]: burn to ash and apply. (Li Shizhen)

**Seed Oil** (obtained by placing [seeds] between two bottles and burning to collect the drippings):
- Malignant sores [惡瘡], scabies [疥], and ringworm [癬].
- When burned as lamp oil, it damages the eyes. (Li Shizhen)

entities_mentioned.name: 木綿 / 古貝 (Mùmián / Gǔbèi) — tree form
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bombax ceiba (silk-cotton tree / red kapok tree)
entities_mentioned.notes: The tree-form mùmián described here — large tree with red camellia-like flowers, large fist-sized fruits containing white floss, found in Jiao and Guang regions — corresponds closely to Bombax ceiba (family Malvaceae/Bombacaceae). Also known as 攀枝花 in modern Chinese.

entities_mentioned.name: 木綿 / 古終 (Mùmián / Gǔzhōng) — herb form
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Gossypium herbaceum or Gossypium arboreum (Levant cotton / tree cotton)
entities_mentioned.notes: The herb-form mùmián — slender-stemmed, yellow flowers resembling mallow, seeds like wutong seeds, cultivated in Jiangnan and Jiangbei — corresponds to cultivated cotton of the genus Gossypium. The spread described (entering Jiangnan at the end of the Song dynasty) is consistent with the historical introduction of Gossypium to central China.

entities_mentioned.name: 娑羅木 (Suōluó tree)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Bombax or Ceiba species, or Shorea robusta (sal tree); context suggests a Bombax-type kapok
entities_mentioned.notes: Suōluó (娑羅) is a Sanskrit-derived term (śāla) sometimes applied to Shorea robusta, but in this context the floss-bearing seeds strongly suggest a Bombax or related genus. Identification is uncertain.

entities_mentioned.name: 白疊 (Báidié)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Gossypium sp. (cotton cloth from Gaochang/Turfan region)
entities_mentioned.notes: Báidié is a well-documented historical term for cotton cloth from Central Asia (Gaochang = modern Turfan, Xinjiang), consistent with Gossypium herbaceum cultivation there.

entities_mentioned.name: 斑枝花 / 攀枝花 (Bānzhīhuā / Pānzhīhuā)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bombax ceiba
entities_mentioned.notes: This is the common folk name for the tree-form kapok in southern China, still in use today.

results.translator_notes: 1. The Sanskrit terms 婆 and 迦羅婆劫 are fragmentary and likely OCR-degraded from fuller transliterations (e.g., 劫波婆 kalpavṛkṣa or similar); they are rendered phonetically as given. 2. The text distinguishes two separate commodities clearly: the tree-form (Bombax ceiba) cultivated in the far south, and the herb-form (Gossypium) which spread northward by the late Song/Yuan period — a historically accurate observation. 3. The gas/flavor entry appears to list two distinct sets of properties (sweet/warm/non-toxic for the floss/cloth; acrid/hot/slightly toxic for the seed oil) that were run together in the source; I have separated them for clarity. 4. The preparation method for seed oil ('two bottles sealed together and burned to collect the dripping oil') is a dry-distillation technique. 5. 金瘡 (jīnchuāng) literally means 'metal wound' — injuries from blades or weapons — rendered as 'sword/metal wounds.' 6. 血崩 (xuè bēng) literally 'blood collapse,' a classical term for abnormal uterine hemorrhage/metrorrhagia.

results.title_inferred: Zuò Wood (Chisel-Handle Tree) — Bark Medicinal Uses
results.original_text:
【釋名】鑿子木。

時珍曰︰此木堅韌，可為鑿柄，故俗名鑿子木。方書皆作柞木，蓋昧此義也。柞乃橡櫟之名，非此木也。

【集解】藏器曰︰柞木生南方，細葉，今之作梳者是也。

時珍曰︰此木處處山中有之，高者丈餘。葉小而有細齒，光滑而韌。其木及葉丫皆有針刺，經冬不凋。五月開碎白花，不結子。其木心理皆白色。

木皮

【氣味】苦，平，無毒。時珍曰︰酸，澀。

【主治】黃膽病，燒末，水服方寸匕，日三（藏器）。治鼠瘻難產，催生利竅，腫毒癰疽（時珍）。
results.translation:
**Explanation of Names:** [Also called] Záozi Wood [Chisel-Handle Wood].

Li Shizhen says: This wood is hard and tough, suitable for making chisel handles; hence its common name Záozi Wood [Chisel-Handle Wood]. Medical texts all write it as Zuò Wood (柞木), but this misses the original meaning. Zuò (柞) is actually the name for oak [Quercus species], not this tree.

**Collected Explanations:** [Chen] Cangqi says: Zuò Wood grows in the southern regions; its leaves are small and fine, and it is the wood presently used to make combs.

Li Shizhen says: This tree is found throughout mountain regions everywhere, and the taller specimens exceed one zhàng [approx. 3 meters] in height. Its leaves are small with fine teeth, smooth, and tough. Both the wood and the leaf axils bear needle-like thorns. It does not shed its leaves in winter. In the fifth month it produces tiny white flowers and does not bear fruit. The heartwood and grain of the wood are entirely white.

**Bark of the Wood**

**Flavor and Nature:** Bitter, balanced [neutral], non-toxic. Li Shizhen says: [Also] sour and astringent.

**Indications and Treatments:**
- Jaundice [huángdǎn disease]: burn [the bark] to powder and take one fāngcùn-bǐ [approx. 1–2 g] dissolved in water, three times daily. (Cangqi)
- Scrofula [rat sores / lǔ fistulas], difficult labor — to promote parturition and open the passages; swollen toxins, carbuncles, and abscesses. (Shizhen)

entities_mentioned.name: 柞木 / 鑿子木 (Zuò Wood / Chisel-Handle Wood)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Xylosma congesta (syn. Xylosma racemosum), family Salicaceae (formerly Flacourtiaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Li Shizhen explicitly distinguishes this plant from the oak (Quercus spp.), which also bears the name 柞 in some texts. The description — small toothed leaves, thorns on wood and leaf axils, small white flowers in the fifth month, no fruit, white heartwood, evergreen — is consistent with Xylosma congesta, a spiny shrub/small tree common in southern China traditionally used for combs.

entities_mentioned.name: 黃膽病 (Jaundice)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Jaundice (hepatic or hemolytic, general)
entities_mentioned.notes: Treated by burning bark to ash/powder and taking orally in water, three times daily.

entities_mentioned.name: 鼠瘻 (Rat Sores / Scrofula)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Scrofula (tuberculous cervical lymphadenitis)
entities_mentioned.notes: A classical Chinese medical term for fistulous sores, often corresponding to scrofula in modern interpretation.

entities_mentioned.name: 癰疽 (Carbuncles and Abscesses)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Suppurative skin infections / deep abscesses
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard classical Chinese term for serious suppurative skin and soft tissue infections.

results.translator_notes: 1. The original text as found in some editions reads 鼠 難產 with a missing character; context and standard Bencao Gangmu editions confirm this should be 鼠瘻 (rat-sore fistula / scrofula), which has been restored in the cleaned text. 2. 方寸匕 (fāngcùn-bǐ) is a classical volumetric measure roughly equivalent to the amount of powder that fits on a square-cun spoon, approximately 1–2 grams depending on the substance. 3. Li Shizhen's philological note distinguishing this 柞木 from the oak 柞 (Quercus) is a characteristic example of his corrective taxonomic commentary throughout the Bencao Gangmu.

results.title_inferred: Evergreen Tree (Budiao Mu) — Monograph
results.original_text:
【集解】藏器曰：生太白山岩谷。樹高二、三尺，葉似槐，莖赤有毛如棠梨，四時不凋。

【氣味】苦，溫，無毒。

【主治】調中補衰，治腰腳，去風氣，卻老變白（藏器）。
results.translation:
【Collected Commentaries】Chen Cangqi states: It grows in the rocky valleys of Mount Taibai. The tree stands two to three chi [roughly 2–3 feet] tall; its leaves resemble those of the scholar tree [Sophora japonica]; its stems are red and bear hairs like those of the crabapple [Malus/Pyrus species]; it does not shed its leaves in any of the four seasons.

【Qi and Flavor】Bitter; warm; non-toxic.

【Indications and Treatments】Regulates and harmonizes the middle [digestive system] and supplements debility; treats pain and weakness of the lower back and legs; dispels wind-qi [rheumatic or arthritic conditions]; retards aging and reverses whitening of the hair. (Chen Cangqi)

entities_mentioned.name: 不凋木 (Budiao Mu, 'Evergreen/Never-Withering Tree')
entities_mentioned.notes: No confident modern botanical identification has been established. The description places it on Mount Taibai (in Shaanxi); its small stature (2–3 chi), Sophora-like leaves, reddish hairy stems, and evergreen habit narrow the field but do not yield a definitive match. Possibly a small evergreen shrub in the Fabaceae or Rosaceae family.

entities_mentioned.name: 槐 (Huai, Scholar Tree)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sophora japonica (now Styphnolobium japonicum)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here only as a morphological comparator for the leaf shape.

entities_mentioned.name: 棠梨 (Tangli, Wild Pear / Crabapple)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pyrus betulifolia or related Malus/Pyrus spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here only as a morphological comparator for the hairy stem texture.

results.translator_notes: The source attribution '藏器' refers to Chen Cangqi (陳藏器), compiler of the Bencao Shiyi (本草拾遺, c. 739 CE), whose findings Li Shizhen frequently incorporates. The therapeutic claim '卻老變白' literally means 'repels old age and reverses [hair turning] white,' i.e., anti-aging and restoring dark hair color — a common trope in Tang-dynasty herbalism. No preparation method is specified in this passage.

results.title_inferred: Maizimu / Selling-Child Wood (賣子木)
results.original_text:
【釋名】買子木。

【集解】恭曰︰賣子木出嶺南、邛州山谷中。其葉似柿。

頌曰︰今惟川西、渠州歲貢，作買子木。木高五、七尺，徑寸許。春生嫩枝條，葉尖，長一、二寸，俱青綠色，枝梢淡紫色。四、五月開碎花，百十枝圍攢作大朵，焦紅色。隨花便生子如椒目，在花瓣中黑而光潔，每株花裁三、五大朵爾。五月採其枝葉用。

時珍曰︰《宋史》渠州貢買子木並子，則子亦當與枝葉同功，而本草缺載，無從考訪。

木

【氣味】甘、微鹹，平，無毒。

【主治】折傷血內溜，續絕補骨髓，止痛安胎（《唐本》）。
results.translation:
**Explanations of Names:** Also written as 買子木 (Maizimu, 'Buying-Child Wood').

**Collected Explanations:** Su Gong states: Maizimu grows in the mountain valleys of Lingnan and Qiongzhou. Its leaves resemble those of the persimmon.

Su Song states: Nowadays it is only Chuanxi [western Sichuan] and Quzhou that present it as an annual tribute, written as 買子木. The tree grows five to seven feet tall, with a trunk diameter of about one cun [~3 cm]. In spring it puts forth tender shoots and branches; the leaves are pointed, one to two cun long, all of a blue-green color, while the tips of the branches are a pale purple. In the fourth and fifth months [May–June] it bears small flowers, with a hundred or more branchlets clustered together into large compound clusters of a scorched-red color. Fruits form immediately after flowering, resembling pepper seeds [椒目], black and glossy within the petals; each plant bears only three to five large flower clusters. The branches and leaves are collected for use in the fifth month [June].

Li Shizhen states: The Song History records that Quzhou submitted 買子木along with its seeds as tribute; thus the seeds ought to share the same medicinal properties as the branches and leaves. However, the materia medica records make no mention of the seeds, and there is no further source from which to investigate the matter.

**Wood**

**Flavor and Nature:** Sweet, slightly salty; neutral; non-toxic.

**Indications and Treatments:** Internal bleeding from fractures and traumatic injuries [血內溜]; reconnecting severed [tendons and bones] and replenishing the bone marrow; relieving pain; and calming the fetus [i.e., preventing miscarriage]. (From the *Tang Bencao* [Tang Materia Medica].)

entities_mentioned.name: 賣子木 / 買子木 (Maizimu)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Myrsine seguinii or a species of Symplocos or Wendlandia uvariifolia (Rubiaceae); identification remains uncertain
entities_mentioned.notes: The description — clusters of small scorched-red flowers, black glossy seeds resembling pepper seeds, leaves resembling persimmon, growing in Lingnan and Sichuan — has led some botanists to suggest Wendlandia uvariifolia (Rubiaceae), but no firm consensus exists. The dual writing of the name (賣 vs. 買, 'selling' vs. 'buying') is noted by Li Shizhen and reflects textual variation across sources.

entities_mentioned.name: 椒目 (pepper seeds)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Seeds of Zanthoxylum bungeanum (Sichuan pepper)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a size and appearance comparator for the fruit of Maizimu, not as a medicinal ingredient in this entry.

results.translator_notes: The alternation between 賣子木 and 買子木 (homophonous characters meaning 'sell' vs. 'buy') reflects a genuine orthographic discrepancy present in historical sources; Li Shizhen flags this implicitly by listing both forms. The medical indication 血內溜 refers to internal extravasation of blood — blood pooling beneath the skin or within the body cavity following traumatic injury — and is rendered accordingly. The source citation 《唐本》 refers to the *Xinxiu Bencao* (新修本草), the Tang dynasty official materia medica, sometimes called the *Tang Bencao*.

results.title_inferred: Mù Tiān Liǎo (Wood Polygonum / Actinidia polygama) — Branches, Leaves, Root, and Fruit
results.original_text:
【釋名】時珍曰：其樹高而味辛如蓼，故名。又馬蓼亦名天蓼而物異。

【集解】恭曰：木天蓼所在皆有，生山谷中。今安州、申州作藤蔓，葉似柘，花白，子如棗許，無定形，中瓤似茄子，味辛，啖之以當薑、蓼。

藏器曰：木蓼，今時所用出山南鳳州。樹高如冬青，不凋。不當以藤天蓼為注，既云木蓼，豈是藤生？自有藤蓼耳。藤蓼生江南、淮南山中，藤著樹生，葉如梨，光而薄，子如棗，即蘇恭以為木天蓼者。又有小天蓼，生天目山、四明山，樹如梔子，冬月不凋，野獸食之。是有三天蓼，俱能逐風，而小者為勝。

頌曰：木天蓼今出信陽。木高二、三丈。三月、四月開花似柘花。五月採子，子作球形似麻，子可藏作果食。蘇恭所說自是藤天蓼也。

時珍曰：天蓼雖有三種，而功用仿佛，蓋一類也。其子可為燭，其芽可食。故陸機云：木蓼為燭，明如胡麻。薛田《詠蜀詩》有「地丁葉嫩和嵐采，天蓼芽新入粉煎」之句。

枝葉

【氣味】辛，溫，有小毒。
甘，溫，無毒。
苦、辛，微熱，無毒。

【主治】症結積聚，風勞虛冷，細切釀酒飲（《唐本》）。
一切風虛羸冷，手足疼痺，無論老幼輕重，浸酒及煮汁服之。十許日，覺皮膚間風出如蟲行（藏器）。
賊風口面喎斜，冷癖氣塊，女子虛勞（甄權）。

根
風蟲牙痛，搗丸塞之，連易四、五次，除根。勿咽汁（時珍，出《普濟》）。

【發明】藏器曰：木天蓼出深山中，人云久服損壽，以其逐風損氣故也。藤天蓼、小天蓼三者，俱能逐風。其中優劣，小者為勝。

子
results.translation:
**Explanation of the Name**
Li Shizhen says: The tree grows tall and its flavor is pungent [xīn] like polygonum [liǎo], hence the name. Note also that Horse Polygonum [mǎ liǎo] is also called Tiān Liǎo, but it is a different plant.

**Collected Commentaries**
Su Gong says: Mù Tiān Liǎo is found everywhere, growing in mountain valleys. In Anzhou and Shenzhou today it grows as a climbing vine. The leaves resemble those of Cudrania [zhè], the flowers are white, and the fruits are roughly the size of jujubes, irregular in shape; the inner flesh resembles eggplant, the flavor is pungent, and it may be eaten as a substitute for ginger and polygonum.

Chen Cangqi says: Mù Liǎo [Wood Polygonum], as currently used in medicine, comes from Fengzhou in Shannan. The tree grows as tall as Chinese holly [dōngqīng] and does not shed its leaves. It should not be glossed as the same as Vine Tiān Liǎo — if it is called a tree [mù], how could it be a vine? There is a separate Vine Polygonum [téng liǎo]. Vine Polygonum grows in the mountains of Jiangnan and Huainan, climbing trees; its leaves resemble those of the pear, smooth and thin; its fruits are like jujubes. This is what Su Gong took to be Mù Tiān Liǎo. There is also a Small Tiān Liǎo, growing on Mount Tianmu and Mount Siming; the tree resembles gardenia [zhīzǐ] and does not shed its leaves in winter; wild animals eat it. Thus there are three kinds of Tiān Liǎo, all capable of expelling wind [qū fēng], but the small variety is the best.

Su Song says: Mù Tiān Liǎo today comes from Xinyang. The tree reaches two to three zhàng in height. It flowers in the third and fourth months, resembling Cudrania flowers. The fruits are harvested in the fifth month; they are spherical, resembling hemp seeds, and can be preserved and eaten as a fruit. What Su Gong described is clearly Vine Tiān Liǎo.

Li Shizhen says: Although there are three kinds of Tiān Liǎo, their functions are similar — they are of the same general class. The fruits can be used as candles, and the young shoots are edible. Lu Ji accordingly wrote: "Mù Liǎo makes candles that burn as bright as sesame." Xue Tian's poem "Ode to Shu" contains the line: "The tender leaves of dìdīng are gathered with the mountain mist; the fresh buds of Tiān Liǎo are fried in a flour batter."

---
**Branches and Leaves**

*Qi and Flavor:* Pungent, warm, slightly toxic. / Sweet, warm, non-toxic. / Bitter and pungent, slightly heating, non-toxic.

*Indications and Treatments:*
- Abdominal masses [zhèng jié], accumulations and gatherings [jī jù], wind taxation [fēng láo] with vacuity and cold: finely chop and brew into wine for drinking. (*Tang Bencao*)
- All conditions of wind vacuity with emaciation and cold, pain and numbness of the hands and feet, regardless of the patient's age or the severity of the condition: steep in wine or decoct the juice for oral administration. Within approximately ten days, the patient will sense wind leaving the skin as though insects were crawling through it. (Chen Cangqi)
- Deviated mouth and face from malign wind [zéi fēng], cold aggregations [lěng pǐ] and qi lumps, vacuity taxation in women. (Zhen Quan)

---
**Root**

*Treatment:* Toothache due to wind or worms: pound into pills and pack into the tooth cavity; replace four or five times in succession to eradicate [the condition]. Do not swallow the juice. (Li Shizhen, citing the *Puji Fang*)

[Condition treated: dental pain (wind/worm toothache). Preparation: root pounded into pill form, applied topically into the tooth cavity, renewed repeatedly.]

---
**Elucidation**
Chen Cangqi says: Mù Tiān Liǎo comes from deep in the mountains. People say that prolonged use shortens life, because it expels wind but in doing so depletes qi. All three — Mù Tiān Liǎo, Vine Tiān Liǎo, and Small Tiān Liǎo — are capable of expelling wind. Among the three, the small variety is the best.

---
**Fruit [Zǐ]**
*(Entry appears to continue in the source but is not given here.)*

results.title_inferred: Fangzhang Wood (Staff-Releasing Wood)
results.original_text:
【集解】藏器曰︰生溫、括、睦、婺諸州山中。樹如木天蓼。老人服之，一月放杖，故以為名。

【氣味】甘，溫，無毒。

【主治】一切風血，理腰腳，輕身變白不老，浸酒服之（藏器）。
results.translation:
【Collected Explanations】Chen Cangqi [藏器] states: It grows in the mountains of Wen, Kuo, Mu, and Wu prefectures. The tree resembles Mu Tianliao [木天蓼, Actinidia polygama]. When elderly people take it, they are able to set aside their walking sticks within one month, hence the name ['Staff-Releasing Wood'].

【Qi and Flavor】Sweet, warm, non-toxic.

【Indications and Treatments】All wind[-related] and blood[-related] conditions; regulates and strengthens the lower back and legs; lightens the body, turns [the hair] white [i.e., reverses graying], and prevents aging. Prepared by steeping in wine and drinking (Chen Cangqi).

entities_mentioned.name: 放杖木 (Fangzhang Mu, 'Staff-Releasing Wood')
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as resembling 木天蓼 (Actinidia polygama). Found in the mountainous regions of Wen (温州), Kuo (括州), Mu (睦州), and Wu (婺州) prefectures, all in present-day Zhejiang province. Specific botanical identity unresolved.

entities_mentioned.name: 木天蓼 (Mu Tianliao)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Actinidia polygama (Silver Vine)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a morphological reference point to describe the appearance of 放杖木, not as the subject plant itself.

results.translator_notes: The phrase '變白不老' is ambiguous: it could mean 'turns white [hair black again]' (i.e., reverses graying) or alternatively 'becomes fair/pale and does not age.' The former interpretation is more common in the genre's rhetorical conventions around rejuvenation. The attributed source 藏器 refers to Chen Cangqi (陳藏器), author of the Bencao Shiyi (本草拾遺, ca. 739 CE). The preparation method—steeping in wine (浸酒服之)—is a standard maceration/tincture technique for this period.

results.title_inferred: Jiegu Wood (Elderberry): Names, Description, and Medical Uses
results.original_text:
【釋名】續骨木（《綱目》）、木蒴藋。

頌曰︰接骨以功而名。花、葉都類蒴藋、陸英、水芹輩，故一名木蒴藋。

【集解】恭曰︰所在皆有之。葉如陸英，花亦相似。但作樹高一、二丈許，木體輕虛無心。斫枝插之便生，人家亦種之。

【氣味】甘、苦，平，無毒。

藏器曰︰搗汁亦吐人，有小毒。

【主治】折傷，續筋骨，除風癢齲齒，可作浴湯（《唐本》）。

根皮︰主痰飲，下水腫及痰瘧，煮汁服之，當利下及吐出。不可多服（藏器）。打傷瘀血，痰瘧，大人七葉，小兒三葉，生搗汁服，取吐（藏器）。
results.translation:
**Names and Appellations**
Also known as Xugu Wood [Bone-Continuing Wood] (Bencao Gangmu) and Mu Shuodi [Tree Elder].

Su Song states: The name 'Jiegu' [Bone-Joining] derives from its therapeutic function. Its flowers and leaves closely resemble those of Shuodi [dwarf elder, Sambucus ebulus], Lu Ying [Chinese elder, Sambucus chinensis], and water parsley [Oenanthe spp.], hence the alternate name Mu Shuodi [Tree Elder].

**Collected Explanations**
Su Gong states: It is found everywhere. The leaves resemble those of Lu Ying, and the flowers are similarly alike. However, it grows as a tree reaching one to two zhang [approximately 3–6 metres] in height, with wood that is light, hollow, and pithy [literally: without a heart]. Cuttings struck from trimmed branches will readily take root, and people also cultivate it in their gardens.

**Flavour and Nature**
Sweet and bitter; neutral [balanced]; non-toxic.

Chen Cangqi states: The juice, when pounded and consumed, can induce vomiting — it has a mild toxicity.

**Indications and Treatments**
Treats fractures and wounds; rejoins sinews and bones; eliminates wind-itch [pruritus] and tooth decay [dental caries]; may be prepared as a bath decoction. (Tang Bencao)

Root bark: Governs phlegm-fluid accumulation [tanyin]; disperses water-swelling [oedema] and phlegm malaria [tanüe]. Boil to extract juice and take orally; it should promote purgation downward and emesis upward. Do not take in excessive amounts. (Chen Cangqi)

For traumatic injury with blood stasis and phlegm malaria: for adults, use seven leaves; for children, use three leaves — pound raw and take the expressed juice orally to induce vomiting. (Chen Cangqi)

entities_mentioned.name: 接骨木 (Jiegu Mu) / 續骨木 / 木蒴藋
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sambucus williamsii or Sambucus racemosa (Chinese/Asian elderberry); possibly also encompassing Sambucus chinensis
entities_mentioned.notes: The description of a tree 1–2 zhang tall, with light pithy wood that propagates easily from cuttings, and flowers resembling Lu Ying (Sambucus chinensis), strongly supports identification as Sambucus williamsii (Chinese red elder) or a closely related Sambucus species.

entities_mentioned.name: 蒴藋 (Shuodi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sambucus ebulus (dwarf elder) or Sambucus chinensis
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a morphological comparator for the plant's flowers and leaves.

entities_mentioned.name: 陸英 (Lu Ying)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sambucus chinensis (Chinese elder)
entities_mentioned.notes: A well-established identification in classical Chinese botanical literature; used here as a comparator.

entities_mentioned.name: 水芹 (Shui Qin)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Oenanthe javanica (Java water dropwort / water parsley)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cited only for morphological comparison of leaf form.

results.translator_notes: The OCR text rendered the plant name 蒴藋 (Shuodi) as '蒴 ' in multiple places — this has been silently corrected to 蒴藋 throughout, as the intended character is unambiguous from context. The term 痰瘧 (tanüe, 'phlegm malaria') refers to a classical Chinese medical category of malarial-type febrile illness accompanied by abundant phlegm. The phrase 木體輕虛無心 ('the wood body is light, hollow, and without a heart/pith') is a standard descriptive formula in Bencao literature indicating soft, pithy, or spongy wood — characteristic of elderberry species. Su Gong (蘇恭) is the Tang-dynasty pharmacologist; Su Song (蘇頌) is the Song-dynasty author of Bencao Tujing; Chen Cangqi (陳藏器) authored Bencao Shiyi. All three are cited as commentators here.

results.title_inferred: Lingshumu (Longevity Staff Wood) — Root Bark Monograph
results.original_text:
【釋名】扶老杖（孟康）、椐。

【集解】藏器曰︰生劍南山谷。圓長皮紫。《漢書》︰孔光年老，賜︰木似竹有節，長不過八、九尺，圍三、四寸，自然有合杖製，不須削理。作杖，令人延年益壽。

時珍曰︰陸氏《詩疏》云︰椐即靈壽也。節中腫，似扶老，即今靈壽也。人以作杖及馬鞭。

弘農郡共北山有之。

根皮

【氣味】苦，平。

【主治】止水（藏器）。
results.translation:
**Explanatory Names:** Fú lǎo zhàng ['staff for supporting the elderly'] (Meng Kang); jū [an archaic plant name].

**Collected Commentaries:** Chen Cangqi [Zangqi] states: It grows in the mountain valleys of Jiannan [present-day Sichuan/Yunnan region]. The stem is round and elongated, with purple bark. According to the *Book of Han*: when Kong Guang was old, he was granted [a staff of this wood]. The wood resembles bamboo in having nodes; it grows no longer than eight or nine feet, with a girth of three to four inches. It is naturally suited to making a walking staff without need for trimming or shaping. Made into a staff, it causes people to live long and enjoy longevity.

Li Shizhen states: Lu Ji's *Commentary on the Odes* [Shi Shu] says: jū is none other than [lingshòu]. The internodes swell outward, resembling a support for the elderly — this is what is today called língshòu mù. People use it to make staffs and horse whips.

It is found in the northern mountains of Hongnong Commandery.

**Root Bark**

**Flavor and Nature:** Bitter; balanced [ping, i.e., neutral in temperature].

**Indications:** Stops [pathological] water [i.e., resolves edema/fluid retention] (Chen Cangqi).

entities_mentioned.name: 靈壽木 (língshòu mù)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Callicarpa giraldii or possibly Aeschynanthus species; more frequently identified as a species of Callicarpa (beautyberry) or alternatively Pteroceltis tatarinowii; identification is debated
entities_mentioned.notes: The description — bamboo-like with swollen nodes, purple bark, naturally staff-shaped, 8–9 feet long — has led some scholars to associate this with Sinarundinaria or a shrubby woody plant of Jiannan. The exact species remains uncertain. The name 椐 (jū) appears in the *Book of Odes* (詩經) and Lu Ji's commentary connects it to this plant.

entities_mentioned.name: 椐 (jū)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Same plant as 靈壽木; archaic poetic name
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced in the *Shi Jing* (Book of Odes) and glossed by Lu Ji as identical to língshòu mù.

entities_mentioned.name: 孔光 (Kong Guang)
entities_mentioned.notes: Historical figure — a Han dynasty official. Mentioned here only as context for the imperial gift of a língshòu mù staff, as recorded in the *Book of Han* (漢書).

results.translator_notes:
1. The OCR appears to have dropped the object of the imperial gift sentence ('賜︰' is followed by a colon with no direct object); the context makes clear that a staff (杖) of this wood was bestowed upon Kong Guang — the missing character is likely '杖' or a similar term. This is flagged as a probable OCR lacuna rather than a textual corruption.
2. '止水' (zhǐ shuǐ) in the therapeutic indication literally means 'stop water' and refers to treating fluid accumulation/edema, a standard term in classical Chinese medicine.
3. The phrase '節中腫' (swelling between nodes) is a diagnostic morphological description of the plant's characteristic internodal bulging, used to justify the folk name 'fú lǎo' (supporting the old).
4. Meng Kang (孟康) was a commentator on the *Book of Han*, cited here as the source for the alternate name 扶老杖.

results.title_inferred: Muma (Wood Hemp) — Medicinal Plant Entry
results.original_text:
【集解】藏器曰：生江南山谷林澤。葉似胡麻相對，山人取以釀酒飲。

【氣味】甘，溫，無毒。

【主治】老血，婦人月閉，風氣羸瘦症瘕。久服，令人有子。
results.translation:
【Collected Explanations】 Chen Cangqi states: It grows in the mountain valleys, forests, and marshes of Jiangnan [the region south of the Yangtze River]. Its leaves resemble those of sesame [Sesamum indicum] and grow in opposite pairs. Mountain dwellers gather it to brew wine for drinking.

【Qi and Flavor】 Sweet; warm; non-toxic.

【Indications and Treatments】 [Used to treat] stagnant old blood, amenorrhea [menstrual closure] in women, wind-qi [wind-related qi disorders] with emaciation, and abdominal masses [zheng-jia accumulations]. Prolonged consumption enables a person to conceive children [promotes fertility].

entities_mentioned.name: 木麻 (Muma)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Uncertain; possibly a species within Urticaceae or a related family given the leaf description resembling sesame and its opposite-leaved growth habit in southern Chinese wetland/forest margins
entities_mentioned.notes: The description 'leaves resembling sesame, growing in opposite pairs' is brief and does not allow confident identification. Chen Cangqi (Zang Qi) is the source cited, suggesting this entry originates from the Tang-era Bencao Shiyi. The name 木麻 ('wood hemp/sesame') is suggestive but not definitively matched to a known species in modern botanical literature.

entities_mentioned.name: 胡麻 (Huma / Sesame)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sesamum indicum
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a morphological comparator for leaf shape, not as the subject plant.

results.translator_notes: The term 症瘕 (zheng-jia) refers to abdominal masses or accumulations, a classical TCM diagnostic category distinguishing fixed (zheng) from movable (jia) masses, often associated with blood stasis or phlegm accumulation. The source cited, 藏器 (Cang Qi), refers to Chen Cangqi (陳藏器), author of the Tang-dynasty Bencao Shiyi (本草拾遺). The therapeutic cluster — old blood stasis, amenorrhea, emaciation, abdominal masses, and infertility — is consistent with a blood-moving, warming herb in classical TCM pharmacology. No preparation method beyond brewing into wine is specified.

results.title_inferred: Dakong (大空) — Root Bark for Killing Lice and Intestinal Worms
results.original_text:
【集解】恭曰︰大空生襄州，所在山谷中亦有之，秦隴人名獨空。作小樹，抽條高六、七尺。葉似楮，小圓濃。根皮赤色。

時珍曰︰小樹大葉，似桐葉而不尖，深綠而皺紋。根皮虛軟，山人采殺虱極妙。搗葉篩蔬圃中，殺蟲。

根皮

【氣味】辛、苦，平，有小毒。

【主治】殺三蟲。作末和油塗發，蟣虱皆死（藏器）。
results.translation:
**Collected Explanations:** Su Gong states: Dakong [大空, 'Great Hollow'] grows in Xiangzhou [modern Hubei/Henan region] and is also found in mountain valleys throughout various localities. The people of Qin and Long [northwest China] call it Dukong [獨空, 'Solitary Hollow']. It forms a small tree, sending up branches to a height of six or seven chi [roughly 6–7 feet]. The leaves resemble those of paper mulberry [楮, Broussonetia papyrifera], being small, rounded, and thick. The root bark is red in color.

Li Shizhen states: It is a small tree with large leaves resembling those of the paulownia [桐] but not pointed at the tip; they are deep green and wrinkled. The root bark is spongy and soft. Mountain dwellers harvest it and find it extremely effective for killing lice. The leaves, when pounded and sifted over vegetable gardens, kill insects.

**Root Bark**

**Flavor and Nature:** Acrid [辛], bitter [苦]; balanced [平]; mildly toxic [有小毒].

**Principal Treatments:** Kills the three types of intestinal worms [三蟲]. Ground into powder and mixed with oil, then applied to the hair, it kills both nits and lice [蟣虱] alike. (Attributed to Chen Cangqi [藏器], i.e., *Bencao Shiyi*.)

entities_mentioned.name: 大空 (Dakong) / 獨空 (Dukong)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Mallotus japonicus or a related Euphorbiaceae species; some scholars suggest Callicarpa species (beautyberry); identification remains uncertain
entities_mentioned.notes: The description of red root bark, large wrinkled paulownia-like leaves, spongy root bark, and antiparasitic properties has led to various proposals. The insecticidal properties and leaf morphology have been compared to some Mallotus or Firmiana species, but no consensus modern identification exists.

entities_mentioned.name: 楮 (Chǔ)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Broussonetia papyrifera (paper mulberry)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a comparative reference for leaf shape.

entities_mentioned.name: 桐 (Tóng)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Paulownia tomentosa or related Paulownia species
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a comparative reference for leaf shape; leaves described as similar but not pointed.

entities_mentioned.name: 三蟲 (sān chóng)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Generic category of intestinal parasites (worms) in traditional Chinese medicine
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical term referring to three classes of internal parasitic worms.

results.translator_notes: The attribution '藏器' refers to Chen Cangqi (陳藏器), Tang dynasty author of the *Bencao Shiyi* (本草拾遺), indicating this therapeutic claim originates from that earlier source. The preparation described — powdering the root bark, mixing with oil, and applying topically to the scalp — is a classic external antiparasitic treatment. 'Three worms' (三蟲) is a TCM taxonomic category rather than a precise parasitological designation. The plant's modern botanical identity is genuinely uncertain; the segment warrants a specialist botanical review before firm identification.

【集解】《別錄》曰：茯苓、茯神生太山山谷大松下。二月、八月採，陰乾。

弘景曰：今出鬱州。大者如三、四升器，外皮黑而細皺，內堅白，形如鳥、獸、龜、鱉者良。虛赤者不佳。性無朽蛀，埋地中三十年，猶色理無異也。

恭曰：今泰山亦有茯苓，實白而塊小，不復採用。第一出華山，形極粗大。雍州南山亦有，不如華山。

保昇曰：所在大松處皆有，惟華山最多。生枯松樹下，形塊無定，以似龜、鳥形者為佳。

禹錫曰：《范子計然》言：茯苓出嵩山及三輔。《淮南子》言：千年之松，下有茯苓，上有菟絲。《典術》言：松脂入地千歲為茯苓，望松樹赤者下有之。《廣志》言：茯神乃松汁所作，勝於茯苓。或云即茯苓貫著松根者。生朱提，濮陽縣。

頌曰：今太、華、嵩山皆有之。出大松下，附根而生，無苗、葉、花、實，作塊如拳在土底，大者至數斤，有赤、白二種。或云松脂變成，或云假松氣而生。今東人見山中古松久為人斬伐，其枯折槎枿，枝葉不復上生者，謂之茯苓。撥即於四面丈餘地內，以鐵頭錐刺地。如有茯苓，則錐固不可拔，乃掘取之。其撥大者，茯苓亦大。皆自作塊，不附著根。其包根而輕虛者為茯神。則假氣生者，其說勝矣。《龜策傳》云：茯苓在菟絲之下，狀如飛鳥之形。新雨已霽，天靜無風，以火夜燒菟絲去之，即篝燭此地罩之，火滅即記其處。明乃掘取，入地四尺或七尺得矣。此類今不聞有之。

宗奭曰：上有菟絲之說，甚為輕信。

時珍曰：下有茯苓，則上有靈氣如絲之狀，山人亦時見之，非虛言也。

【氣味】甘，平，無毒。

元素曰：性溫，味甘而淡，氣味俱薄，浮而升，陽也。

之才曰：馬藺為之使。得甘草、防風、芍藥、紫石英、麥門冬，共療五臟。惡白蘞，畏牡蒙、地榆、雄黃、秦艽、龜甲，忌米醋及酸物。

弘景曰：藥無馬間，或是馬莖也。

恭曰：李氏《本草》：馬刀為茯苓使。間字草書似刀字，傳訛爾。

志曰：二注恐皆非也。當是馬藺字。

【主治】胸脅逆氣，憂恚驚邪恐悸，心下結痛，寒熱煩滿咳逆，口焦舌乾，利小便。久服，安魂養神，不飢延年（《本經》）。止消渴好睡，大腹淋瀝，膈中痰水，水腫淋結，開胸腑，調臟氣，伐腎邪，長陰，益氣力，保神氣（《別錄》）。開胃止嘔逆，善安心神，主肺痿痰壅，心腹脹滿，小兒驚癇，女人熱淋（甄權）。補五勞七傷，開心益志，止健忘，暖腰膝，安胎（大明）。止渴，利小便，除濕益燥，和中益氣，利腰臍間血（元素）。逐水緩脾，生津導氣，平火止泄，除虛熱，開腠理（李杲）。瀉膀胱，益脾胃，治腎積奔豚（好古）。
破結氣（甄權）。瀉心、小腸、膀胱濕熱，利竅行水（時珍）。
水腫膚脹，開水道，開腠理（時珍）。
辟不祥，療風眩風虛，五勞口乾，止驚悸、多恚怒、善忘，開心益智，安魂魄，養精神（《別錄》）。補勞乏，主心下急痛堅滿。人虛而小腸不利者，加而用之（甄權）。
偏風、口面喎斜、毒風、筋攣不語、心神驚掣、虛而健忘（甄權）。治腳氣、痺痛，諸筋牽縮（時珍）。

【發明】弘景曰：茯苓白色者補，赤色者利。俗用甚多，仙方服食亦為至要。云其通神而致靈，和魂而煉魄，利竅而益肌，厚腸而開心，調營而理衛，上品仙藥也。善能斷谷不飢。

宗奭曰：茯苓行水之功多，益心脾不可缺也。

元素曰：茯苓赤瀉白補，上古無此說。氣味俱薄，性浮而升。其用有五：利小便也；開腠理也；生津液也；除虛熱也；止瀉也。如小便利或數者，多服則損人目。汗多人服之，亦損元氣，夭人壽，為其淡而滲也。又云：淡為天之陽，陽當上行，何以利水而瀉下？氣薄者陽中之陰，所以茯苓利水瀉下。不離陽之體，故入手太陽。

杲曰：白者入壬癸，赤者入丙丁。味甘而淡，降也，陽中陰也。其用有六：利竅而除濕，益氣而和中，治驚悸，生津液，小便多者能止，小便結者能通。又云：濕淫所勝，小便不利。淡以利竅，甘以助陽。甘平能益脾逐水，乃除濕之聖藥也。

好古曰：白者入手太陰、足太陽、少陽經氣分，赤者入足太陰、手少陰、太陽氣分。伐腎邪。小便多，能止之。",

【釋名】江珠。

時珍曰︰虎死則精魄入地化為石，此物狀似之，故謂之虎魄。俗文從玉，以其類玉也。梵書謂之阿濕摩揭婆。

【集解】《別錄》曰︰琥珀生永昌。

弘景曰︰舊說松脂淪入地千年所化。今燒之亦作松氣。亦有中有一蜂，形色如生者。《博物志》乃云“燒蜂巢所作”，恐非實也。此或蜂為松脂所沾，因墜地淪沒爾。亦有煮毈雞子及青魚枕作者，並非真。惟以手心摩熱拾芥為真。今並從外國來，而出茯苓處並無，不知出琥珀處複有茯苓否也？

珣曰︰琥珀是海松木中津液，初若桃膠，後乃凝結。複有南珀，不及舶上來者。

保升曰︰楓脂入地千年變為琥柏，不獨松脂變也。大抵木脂入地千年皆化，但不及楓、松有脂而多經年歲爾。蜂巢既燒，安有蜂形尚在其間？

宗奭曰︰今西戎亦有，其色差淡而明澈。南方者色深而重濁，彼土人多碾為物形。若謂千年茯苓所化，則其沾著蜂、蟻宛然具在，極不然也。《地理志》云︰海南林邑多出琥珀，松脂淪入地所化。有琥珀則旁無草木。入土淺者五尺，深者八、九尺。大者如斛，削去皮乃成。此說為勝。但土地有所宜、不宜，故有能化、不化。燒蜂之說，不知何據？

承曰︰諸家所說茯苓、琥珀，雖有小異同，皆云松脂所化。但茯苓、茯神，乃大松

【釋名】瑿珀。

斆曰︰瑿是眾珀之長，故號瑿珀。

時珍曰︰亦作䃜。其色黳黑，故名。

【集解】恭曰︰古來相傳松脂千年為茯苓，又千年為琥珀，又千年為瑿。二物燒之皆有松氣。狀似玄玉而輕。出西戎，而有茯苓處無此物。今西州南三百里黑磧中得者，大則方尺，黑潤而輕，燒之腥臭。高昌人名為木瑿，謂玄玉為石瑿。共州土石間得者，燒作松氣，功同琥珀，見風拆破，不堪為器。恐此二種及琥珀，或非松脂所為也。慎微曰︰《梁公子傳》︰奈公云︰交河之間平磧中，掘深一丈，下有瑿珀，黑逾純漆，或大如車輪。末服，攻婦人小腸癥瘕諸疾。時珍曰︰瑿即琥珀之黑色者，或因土色熏染，或是一種木瀋結成，未必是千年琥珀復化也。《玉策經》言︰松脂千年作茯苓，茯苓千年作琥珀，琥珀千年作石膽，石膽千年作威喜。大抵皆是神異之說，未可深憑。雷斆琥珀下所說諸珀可據。

【氣味】甘，平，無毒。

【主治】補心安神，破血生肌，治婦人癥瘕（《唐本》）。小兒帶之辟惡，磨滴目翳赤障（藏器）。
results.translation:
**Explanation of Names.** Also called Yipò [Yi-amber].

Lei Xiao states: Yi is the foremost among all ambers, hence it is called Yipò.

Li Shizhen states: It is also written as 䃜 [an alternate character]. Its color is jet-black [黳黑], hence the name.

**Collected Explanations.** Su Gong states: It has long been transmitted that pine resin after one thousand years becomes Poria fungus [茯苓], after another thousand years becomes amber [琥珀], and after yet another thousand years becomes Yi. Both substances, when burned, emit a pine-like odor. In form they resemble dark jade [玄玉] but are lighter. Yi comes from the Western Rong [western frontier peoples], and in places where Poria grows, this substance is not found. That which is now obtained in the black gravel desert three hundred li south of Xizhou — at its largest it measures a square foot, is black, lustrous, and light, and when burned emits a rank, foul smell. The people of Gaochang call it Mù-yī [wood-yi], while they call dark jade Shí-yī [stone-yi]. That which is obtained from among the earth and stones of Gongzhou, when burned, gives off a pine-like odor, its efficacy is the same as amber, but it cracks and breaks upon exposure to wind and cannot be fashioned into implements. I suspect that these two varieties, as well as amber itself, may not be derived from pine resin at all. Tang Shenwei states: The Lianggongzi zhuan records that a certain Master Nai said: In the flat gravel plains between the rivers of Jiaohe, if one digs down one zhang [approximately 3 meters], Yi-amber is found below — blacker than pure lacquer, and some pieces as large as a cart wheel. Taken in powdered form, it treats masses [癥] and accumulations [瘕] and various ailments of the small intestine in women.

Li Shizhen states: Yi is simply a black-colored variety of amber. It may have become darkened through being stained by the color of the surrounding soil, or it may be formed from the congealed sap [木瀋] of a particular kind of tree; it is not necessarily amber of one thousand years' age that has transformed further. The Yuce Jing [Classic of Jade Tallies] states: Pine resin after one thousand years becomes Poria fungus; Poria fungus after one thousand years becomes amber; amber after one thousand years becomes bile-stone [石膽]; bile-stone after one thousand years becomes Weixi [威喜, a type of mineral]. These are for the most part miraculous and fantastical claims and cannot be relied upon deeply. The various ambers described by Lei Xiao under the amber entry may be consulted as a credible reference.

**Flavor and Nature.** Sweet; balanced [平]; non-toxic.

**Indications and Treatment.** Nourishes the heart and calms the spirit; breaks up [stagnant] blood and promotes tissue regeneration; treats abdominal masses and accumulations [癥瘕] in women (Tang Materia Medica [《唐本》]). Worn by children, it wards off malign influences; ground and applied as drops, it treats eye nebulae [目翳] and red visual obstructions [赤障] (Chen Cangqi [藏器]).

entities_mentioned.name: 瑿 / 瑿珀 (Yī / Yīpò)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Black amber; likely a dark variety of fossilized resin (amber), possibly jet or a resinite darkened by burial conditions
entities_mentioned.notes: Li Shizhen himself expresses skepticism about the legendary origin story. Modern interpretation would treat this as dark or black amber, potentially jet (lignite/gagat) or a heavily oxidized resinite. The text distinguishes two subtypes: 木瑿 (wood-yi, from organic/resinous origin) and 石瑿 (stone-yi, dark jade).

entities_mentioned.name: 茯苓 (Fúlíng)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Poria cocos (Schwein.) F.A. Wolf — Poria fungus / Indian bread
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned in the legendary transformation sequence: pine resin → Poria → amber → Yi.

entities_mentioned.name: 琥珀 (Hǔpò)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Amber — fossilized tree resin, primarily from conifers
entities_mentioned.notes: Amber serves as the conceptual parent substance to Yi in the text's cosmological sequence.

entities_mentioned.name: 石膽 (Shídǎn)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Chalcanthite (copper sulfate pentahydrate, CuSO₄·5H₂O)
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned in the Yuce Jing transformation sequence.

entities_mentioned.name: 威喜 (Wēixǐ)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Unknown mineral; possibly a legendary or cosmological substance
entities_mentioned.notes: Cited only as the final stage in the Yuce Jing transformation chain. Not clearly identified in modern mineralogy.

results.translator_notes: 1. The passage contains a notable internal critical voice: both Su Gong and Li Shizhen explicitly question the traditional transformation mythology (pine resin → Poria → amber → Yi), which is unusually skeptical for a Bencao entry. 2. The medical applications extracted: (a) Name: 瑿/瑿珀; Condition: abdominal masses and accumulations (癥瘕) in women, specifically of the small intestine; Preparation: ground into powder and taken orally (末服). (b) Condition: pediatric warding of malign influences; Preparation: worn as an amulet. (c) Condition: eye nebulae (目翳) and red visual obstructions (赤障); Preparation: ground and instilled as eye drops. 3. '黑磧' refers to a dark desert or gravel plain; rendered as 'black gravel desert.' 4. The reference '《唐本》' denotes the Tang Bencao (Tang Materia Medica, 659 CE), and '藏器' refers to Chen Cangqi, author of the Bencao Shiyi (c. 741 CE). 5. '雷斆' is Lei Xiao, author of the Leigong Paozhi Lun on pharmaceutical processing.

【釋名】豭豬屎（《本經》）、豕橐（《莊子》）、地烏桃（《圖經》）。

弘景曰：其塊黑似豬屎，故以名之。司馬彪注《莊子》云：豕橐一名苓，其根似豬矢。是也。

時珍曰：馬屎曰通，豬屎曰零（即苓字），其塊零落而下故也。

【集解】《別錄》曰：豬苓生衡山山谷，及濟陰冤句。二月、八月採，陰乾。

弘景曰：是楓樹苓，其皮黑色，肉白而實者佳，削去皮用。

頌曰：今蜀州、眉州亦有之。生土底，不必楓根下始有也。

時珍曰：豬苓亦是木之餘氣所結，如鬆之餘氣結茯苓之義。他木皆有，楓木為多耳。

【氣味】甘，平，無毒。

普曰︰神農︰甘。雷公︰苦，無毒。權曰︰微熱。元素曰︰氣平味甘，氣味俱薄，升而微降，與茯苓同。

杲曰︰淡、甘，平，降也，陽中陰也。好古曰︰甘重於苦，陽也。入足太陽、足少陰經。

【主治】痎瘧，解毒、蠱疰、不祥，利水道。久服，輕身耐老（《本經》）。解傷寒、溫疫、大熱，發汗，主腫脹滿、腹急痛（甄權）。治渴除濕，去心中懊憹（元素）。瀉膀胱（好古）。開腠理，治淋腫腳氣，白濁帶下，妊娠子淋胎腫，小便不利（時珍）。

【發明】頌曰︰張仲景治消渴脈浮、小便不利、微熱者，豬苓散發其汗。病欲飲水而複吐，名為水逆，冬時寒嗽如瘧狀者，亦與豬苓散，此即五苓散也。豬苓、茯苓、朮各三分，澤瀉五分，桂二分，細搗篩，水服方寸匕，日三。多飲暖水，汗出即愈。利水道諸湯劑，無若此快，今人皆用之。

杲曰︰苦以泄滯，甘以助陽，淡以利竅，故能除濕利小便。

宗奭曰︰豬苓行水之功多，久服必損腎氣，昏人目。久服者宜詳審之。

元素曰︰豬苓淡滲，大燥亡津液，無濕癥者勿服之。

時珍曰：豬苓淡滲，氣升而又能降。故能開腠理，利小便，與茯苓同功。但入補藥不如茯苓也。
results.translation:
**Explanation of Names**
Aliases: *Jiazhu shi* [boar's dung] (Shennong Bencao Jing), *shi tuo* [pig's sack] (Zhuangzi), *di wu tao* [earth black-peach] (Tujing).

Tao Hongjing [Hongjing] states: Its lumps are black and resemble pig dung, hence the name. Sima Biao's commentary on the Zhuangzi says: '*Shi tuo* is also called *ling*; its roots resemble pig droppings.' This is indeed the same thing.

Li Shizhen [Shizhen] states: Horse dung is called *tong* [通]; pig dung is called *ling* [零, i.e., the character *ling* 苓], because the lumps scatter and fall downward, hence the name.

**Collected Commentaries**
Bielu states: Zhu ling grows in the mountain valleys of Hengshan and in Jiyin Yuanju. It is gathered in the second and eighth months and dried in the shade.

Hongjing states: It is the fungus of the maple tree [楓樹苓]. Those with a black skin and white, solid flesh are the finest; the skin is pared away before use.

Su Song [Song] states: Today it is also found in Shuzhou and Meizhou. It grows underground and need not necessarily grow only beneath maple tree roots.

Shizhen states: Zhu ling is likewise formed from the residual vital energy [餘氣] of trees, in the same way that pine trees' residual energy gives rise to fu ling [Poria cocos]. It can form on other trees as well, but the maple tree is most common.

**Qi and Flavor**
Sweet, neutral, non-toxic.

Zhu Pu [Pu] states — Shennong: sweet. Lei Gong: bitter, non-toxic. [Chen] Quan states: mildly warming. [Zhang] Yuansu states: its qi is neutral and its flavor sweet; both qi and flavor are thin [i.e., not strongly concentrated]; it rises and slightly descends — the same as fu ling.

Li Gao [Gao] states: bland and sweet, neutral; it descends; it is yin within yang. Wang Haogu [Haogu] states: sweetness predominates over bitterness; it is yang. It enters the foot taiyang and foot shaoyin channels.

**Indications and Treatments**
— Malarial fevers [痎瘧], detoxification, [treatment of] gu-poisoning [蠱疰] and inauspicious conditions, and promoting the flow of the water pathways. Long-term use lightens the body and retards aging. (Shennong Bencao Jing)
— Resolving cold-damage [傷寒] and warm-epidemic [溫疫] fevers, high fever, inducing sweating; governing swelling and abdominal distension, and acute abdominal pain. (Zhen Quan)
— Treating thirst and eliminating dampness; relieving a distressed, vexed sensation in the heart [心中懊憹]. (Zhang Yuansu)
— Draining the bladder. (Wang Haogu)
— Opening the interstices of the skin and flesh [腠理]; treating strangury [淋] with swelling, leg qi [腳氣, beriberi], white turbid discharge and leukorrhea [白濁帶下], urinary strangury and fetal edema in pregnancy, and difficult urination [小便不利]. (Li Shizhen)

**Elaborations and Discussion**
Su Song states: Zhang Zhongjing, in treating wasting-thirst [消渴] with a floating pulse, difficult urination, and mild fever, used Zhu Ling Powder [豬苓散] to induce sweating. When a patient desires water but vomits it up — a condition called 'water reversal' [水逆] — or when there is a cold cough in winter resembling malarial episodes, he also administered Zhu Ling Powder, which is in fact the Five-Ling Powder [五苓散]. The formula: zhu ling, fu ling, and atractylodes [朮], three parts each; ze xie [Alisma], five parts; gui [cinnamon], two parts. Pound finely and sieve; take one square-cun spoonful [方寸匕] with water, three times daily. Drink plenty of warm water; once sweating occurs, recovery follows. Among all decoctions that promote the water pathways, none acts as swiftly as this; it is in common use today.

Li Gao states: Its bitterness drains stagnation, its sweetness supports yang, its blandness opens the orifices — thus it eliminates dampness and promotes urination.

Kou Zongshi [Zongshi] states: Zhu ling has great power in moving water. Long-term use will inevitably damage kidney qi and dim the eyes. Those who use it long-term should deliberate carefully.

Zhang Yuansu states: Zhu ling is bland and percolating [淡滲]; it greatly dries out and depletes fluids. Those who have no dampness-pattern [濕癥] should not take it.

Shizhen states: Zhu ling is bland and percolating; its qi rises yet can also descend. Thus it opens the interstices of skin and flesh and promotes urination — its function is the same as fu ling. However, as an ingredient in tonifying formulas it is inferior to fu ling.

entities_mentioned.name: 豬苓 (Zhu Ling)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Polyporus umbellatus (Pers.) Fr. — the sclerotium of the umbrella polypore fungus
entities_mentioned.notes: A medicinal fungus parasitic on the roots of maple and other trees. The sclerotium is the part used medicinally. Well-established in modern Chinese pharmacopoeia (Polyporus umbellatus). Contains ergosterol and polysaccharides; pharmacologically confirmed as a diuretic.

entities_mentioned.name: 五苓散 (Wu Ling San / Five-Ling Powder)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical TCM formula: Polyporus umbellatus, Poria cocos, Atractylodes macrocephala, Alisma orientalis, Cinnamomum cassia
entities_mentioned.notes: Zhang Zhongjing formula from the Shang Han Lun. The text quotes the formula with proportions: zhu ling, fu ling, and zhu (atractylodes) 3 parts each; ze xie 5 parts; gui (cinnamon) 2 parts. Used for urinary difficulty, fluid retention, and 'water reversal' vomiting.

entities_mentioned.name: 茯苓 (Fu Ling)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Poria cocos (Schw.) Wolf — the sclerotium of Wolfiporia extensa
entities_mentioned.notes: Compared throughout as an analogous fungal sclerotium forming from residual tree energy, particularly of pines. Considered superior to zhu ling for use in tonic formulas.

entities_mentioned.name: 澤瀉 (Ze Xie)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Alisma plantago-aquatica / Alisma orientalis
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a component of Five-Ling Powder (five parts).

entities_mentioned.name: 朮 (Zhu)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Atractylodes macrocephala or A. lancea
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a component of Five-Ling Powder (three parts).

entities_mentioned.name: 桂 (Gui)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cinnamomum cassia — cinnamon bark
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a component of Five-Ling Powder (two parts).

results.translator_notes: 1. '方寸匕' (fang cun bi) is a classical volumetric measure — a spoon approximately 1 square cun (roughly 2–3 grams of powder); translated literally as 'square-cun spoonful' with brackets for clarity. 2. '心中懊憹' (xin zhong ao nao) describes a subjective sensation of distress, vexation, or a troubled, oppressive feeling in the chest/epigastrium — a recognized TCM symptom term rendered as 'distressed, vexed sensation in the heart.' 3. '腠理' (cou li) refers to the interstices of the skin, flesh, and viscera through which qi and fluids are thought to flow; retained in translation with a gloss. 4. The commentators cited are: Tao Hongjing (456–536 CE), Su Song (1020–1101 CE), Li Gao (1180–1251 CE), Wang Haogu (13th c.), Zhang Yuansu (12th–13th c.), Kou Zongshi (fl. 1116 CE), Zhen Quan (541–643 CE), and Zhu Pu (3rd c. CE). 5. '痎瘧' refers specifically to intermittent or chronic malarial-type fevers, distinguished from ordinary 瘧疾; translated as 'malarial fevers.'

**[Names and Nomenclature]**
Alternate names: Jixie [寄屑, 'lodging shavings'] (Shennong Bencao Jing); Yumu [寓木, 'guest wood'] (Shennong Bencao Jing). Li Shizhen states: This plant lodges and grows upon other trees, like a bird perched above, hence it is called Jisheng ['parasitic growth'], Yumu ['guest wood'], and Diaonu [蔦木, 'clinging wood']. Commonly it is called Jisheng Cao ['mistletoe herb']. The Dongfang Shuo Zhuan states: [text missing/incomplete in source].

**[Collected Commentaries]**
The Mingyi Bielu states: Mulberry mistletoe [Sang Shang Jisheng] grows on mulberry trees in the river valleys of Hongnong. The stems are collected on the third day of the third month.

Tao Hongjing states: Mistletoe is found on pine, poplar, and maple trees as well; the forms are generally similar, but they differ according to the host tree from which they draw their sap, and each is named after its respective host tree. It grows between branches, with roots embedded within the branch nodes. The leaves are round, greenish-red, thick, lustrous, and easily broken. Side branches grow spontaneously from the nodes. It grows through winter and summer; flowers appear in the fourth month and are white. Fruits ripen in the fifth month and are red, about the size of small beans [adzuki beans]. It is found everywhere, but the variety from Pengcheng is considered superior. People commonly call it Xuduan [續斷, 'Dipsacus'] and use it as such, but in the Shennong Bencao Jing, Xuduan is separately listed in the upper grade [of herbs] and has different therapeutic indications. Merchants mix them together without discernment.

Su Gong states: This plant most commonly grows on maple, oak [Quercus dentata], Zelkova-willow [Juglans-willow type], and water-poplar trees. The leaves have no distinction of upper and lower surface [i.e., are similar on both sides], resembling fine willow leaves but thicker and more brittle. The stems are thick and short. The fruits are yellow, about the size of small jujubes. Only in Guozhou is there a variety that grows on mulberry trees; its fruit juice is very sticky, the seed is as large as a small bean, and it ripens in the ninth month, being yellow in colour. Tao's claim that the fruits are red in the fifth month and the size of small beans is evidently based on not having seen the actual plant. People in Jiangnan have traditionally used its stems as Xuduan, which is altogether incorrect.

Han Baosheng states: Mistletoe is found on many kinds of trees, and the stems and leaves are all similar. It is said that birds eat the seeds of a certain plant and their droppings fall onto trees, where the plant germinates from the vital emanation [qi] of the host. The leaves resemble those of the tangerine but are thicker and softer; the stems resemble those of the pagoda tree [Sophora japonica] but are plumper and more brittle. Although it is found everywhere, only the variety growing on mulberry is considered good. However, unless one gathers it personally, it is difficult to distinguish. One may cut the stem and examine it: a deep yellow colour is the distinguishing mark. The Tujing [Illustrated Classic] further states: the leaves resemble those of Gentiana [longdan, 龍膽] but are thicker and broader. The stem is short and resembles a chicken's foot, giving it a tree-like form. It flowers in the third and fourth months with yellow-white blossoms. Fruits form in the sixth and seventh months, are yellow-green, about the size of small beans; those with thick and sticky juice are considered best.

Da Ming [Ri Huazi] states: People commonly collect specimens growing on Zelkova trees and pass them off as mulberry mistletoe. Those actually found on mulberry are extremely rare, and even when found, their form differs from those on Zelkova trees. Second in quality are those on maple trees, which are equal in efficacy to those on Zelkova and are yellow in colour. They are harvested in the seventh and eighth months.

Kou Zongshi states: It is universally claimed that mulberry mistletoe is found everywhere. Yet in all my travels in official service north and south, I have found it nowhere easy to obtain. Could it be that annual cutting and trampling prevents it from growing? Or that regional conditions differ? If one accepts the explanation that birds drop seeds into branch nodes where the plant then grows from absorbed qi, then wheat ought to produce wheat, and millet ought to produce millet — not this single distinct plant. Rather, it absorbs the vital transformative qi [of heaven and earth] and is a distinct thing of its own. The ancients selected only that growing on mulberry, to borrow the qi of that tree. Precisely because the genuine article is hard to obtain — when the genuine article is swallowed, the effect is certain, like... [text breaks off].

**[Qi and Flavour]**
Bitter, balanced [ping], non-toxic.
The Mingyi Bielu states: Sweet, non-toxic.
[Also recorded as:] Sweet, balanced, non-toxic.

**[Indications and Treatments]**
- Lumbar pain, stiffness of the back in children, abscesses and swellings [yongzhong], enriching the skin and flesh, strengthening the hair and teeth, promoting growth of beard and eyebrows, calming the fetus [Shennong Bencao Jing].
- Eliminating flooding and spotting [崩中, metrorrhagia] in women due to internal damage and deficiency, residual ailments after childbirth, promoting lactation, treating metal-weapon wounds [jinchuang], and dispelling impediment-syndrome [bi] [Mingyi Bielu].
- Strengthening the sinews and bones, and benefiting the blood vessels [Da Ming].
- Treating incessant bleeding during pregnancy [漏血不止] and securing the fetus [Zhen Quan].
- Brightening the eyes, lightening the body, and penetrating the spirit [Shennong Bencao Jing].

results.translator_notes:
1. The 釋名 section ends abruptly mid-citation of the Dongfang Shuo Zhuan, and the 集解 entry from Bielu is also incomplete — the stem-collection passage cuts off. These are lacunae in the source text (likely OCR truncation or original manuscript damage), not translation omissions.
2. Kou Zongshi's commentary also breaks off mid-sentence at the end ('真者下咽，必驗如…'), indicating a further lacuna.
3. The term 金瘡 (jinchuang) literally means 'metal-weapon wound' and refers to cuts from blades or arrows; translated accordingly.
4. 崩中 (bengzhong) is a classical gynaecological term for sudden heavy uterine bleeding (metrorrhagia/flooding); 漏血 (louxue) refers to slow or persistent spotting during pregnancy.
5. The philosophical digression by Kou Zongshi on whether bird-dropped seeds could produce a different species reflects a genuine early debate about plant generation and botanical identity — notable as a pre-modern empirical argument.
6. 氣味 section lists the flavour as 'bitter' first, then separately as 'sweet' from Bielu and another unnamed source; this discrepancy is preserved as in the original.

results.title_inferred: Fēng Liǔ (Maple-Willow / Parasitic Plant on Maple) — Bark Medicinal Uses
results.original_text:
【集解】恭曰︰楓柳出原州。葉似槐，莖赤根黃。子六月熟，綠色而細。剝取莖皮用。

時珍曰︰蘇恭言楓柳有毒，出原州。陳藏器駁之，以為楓柳皮即今楓樹皮，性澀能止水痢。按︰《斗門方》言即今楓樹上寄生，其葉亦可製粉霜，此說是也。若是楓樹，則處處甚多，何必專出原州耶？陳說誤矣。楓皮見前楓香脂下。

皮

【氣味】辛，大熱，有毒。

【主治】風，齲齒痛（《唐本》）。積年痛風不可忍，久治無效者。

細銼焙，不限多少，入腦、麝浸酒常服，以醉為度（《斗門方》）。
results.translation:
**Collected Explanations**

Su Gong [Tang-era pharmacologist] states: Fēng liǔ [楓柳] originates from Yuanzhou. Its leaves resemble those of the pagoda tree [Sophora japonica], its stems are red, and its roots are yellow. The seeds ripen in the sixth month; they are green and small. The stem bark is stripped off for use.

Li Shizhen [author] states: Su Gong says that fēng liǔ is toxic and comes from Yuanzhou. Chen Cangqi [Tang pharmacologist] refuted this, holding that the bark of fēng liǔ is simply the bark of the common maple tree [fēng shù], which has an astringent nature and can stop watery dysentery. However, according to the *Doumen Fang* [a formula collection], the plant in question is actually a parasitic growth [寄生] on the maple tree, and its leaves can also be used to prepare fěn shuāng [sublimed calomel-type powder]. This latter explanation is correct. If fēng liǔ were simply the maple tree itself, it would be abundant everywhere — why would it be said to come specifically from Yuanzhou? Chen's interpretation is mistaken. For maple bark [楓皮] itself, see the preceding entry on maple resin [楓香脂].

---

**Bark**

**Flavor and Nature:** Acrid [xīn], greatly hot [dà rè], toxic.

**Indications and Treatment:**
- Wind[-related ailments]; toothache from dental caries [齲齒痛]. (*Tanben* [*Tang Bencao*])
- Chronic, unbearable gout [痛風] of many years' standing, in cases where prolonged treatment has proven ineffective.

*Preparation method:* Finely chop and dry-roast [the bark] in any quantity. Steep together with borneol [腦, i.e., bīng piàn] and musk [麝, i.e., shè xiāng] in wine and take regularly, drinking until intoxicated. (*Doumen Fang*)

entities_mentioned.name: 楓柳 (fēng liǔ)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Likely a parasitic or epiphytic plant growing on maple trees; possibly a mistletoe species (Loranthaceae or Viscaceae) or a related hemiparasite. Not a willow or maple itself.
entities_mentioned.notes: Li Shizhen argues it is a parasite on the maple tree, not the maple tree itself, and not a willow. The precise botanical identity remains uncertain; the common name literally combines 'maple' and 'willow,' which has caused historical confusion.

entities_mentioned.name: 楓樹 (fēng shù, maple tree)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Liquidambar formosana (Chinese sweet gum) or Acer spp., depending on context; in Bencao literature 楓 most often refers to Liquidambar formosana.
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced here as the host tree and as a source of confusion with the subject drug.

entities_mentioned.name: 腦 (nǎo, borneol)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Borneol (bornane-2-ol), derived from Dryobalanops aromatica or Blumea balsamifera
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as an ingredient in the wine maceration formula.

entities_mentioned.name: 麝 (shè, musk)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Moschus spp. (musk deer secretion)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as an ingredient in the wine maceration formula.

entities_mentioned.name: 粉霜 (fěn shuāng)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A sublimed mercurous chloride-type powder (calomel); in classical Chinese pharmacy, fěn shuāng refers to a white crystalline powder obtained by sublimation, often mercury-based.
entities_mentioned.notes: The text claims the leaves of fēng liǔ can be used to produce fěn shuāng, which is an unusual claim and may indicate a processing/extractive use of the plant rather than a mercury-based compound per se.

results.translator_notes: 1. The entry hinges on a bibliographic dispute: Su Gong (Tang Bencao), Chen Cangqi (Bencao Shiyi), and the Doumen Fang give conflicting identifications, and Li Shizhen sides with the Doumen Fang's view that fēng liǔ is a parasite on the maple, not the maple itself. 2. 'Yuanzhou' (原州) is a historical prefecture in what is now the Ningxia/Gansu region of northwest China, which would indeed make the distribution narrower than that of common maple trees. 3. The therapeutic use for chronic gout via wine maceration with borneol and musk is noteworthy as an early recorded treatment for arthritis/gout. 4. The phrase 以醉為度 ('drink to the point of intoxication') indicates that wine volume was not fixed but determined by the patient's tolerance — a common instruction in classical formula literature.

【氣味】苦，辛，無毒。

【主治】小兒中蠱毒，腹內堅痛，面目青黃，淋露骨立。取二兩為末，如茶點服，日四、五服（時珍。《聖惠方》）。
results.translation:
**Flavor and Nature:** Bitter, acrid [辛], non-toxic.

**Principal Treatments:** For children afflicted with gu-poison [蠱毒; a category of toxic parasitic or magical poisoning], presenting with hardness and pain in the abdomen, a bluish-yellow complexion of the face and eyes, emaciation, and wasting away until the bones show prominently. Take two liang [兩], grind into powder, and administer as one would take tea [i.e., dissolved in hot water as a draft]; give four to five doses per day. (Li Shizhen, citing the *Shenghuifang* [聖惠方, 'Prescriptions for Universal Relief'].)

entities_mentioned.name: 桃寄生 (Táo jìshēng)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Loranthus or Viscum species parasitic on Prunus persica (peach tree); possibly Taxillus chinensis or a related Loranthaceae/Santalaceae member growing on peach
entities_mentioned.notes: The term 寄生 (jìshēng) generically denotes a parasitic plant (mistletoe or similar). When qualified by 桃 (peach), it refers specifically to the parasitic plant growing on peach trees. Exact species-level identification is uncertain; Loranthaceae members are the most likely candidates in the Chinese materia medica context.

entities_mentioned.name: 蠱毒 (gǔ dú)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: null
entities_mentioned.notes: A classical Chinese medical and cultural concept encompassing a range of conditions attributed to parasitic organisms, toxic substances, or malevolent magical poisoning. In clinical terms, symptoms described (abdominal hardness, jaundiced/cyanotic complexion, severe wasting) may correspond to conditions such as parasitic infection, severe liver disease, or malnutrition in children.

entities_mentioned.name: 《聖惠方》 (Shènghuì Fāng)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Taiping Shenghuifang (太平聖惠方), a major official Song dynasty pharmacopoeia compiled c. 992 CE
entities_mentioned.notes: A 100-volume imperial medical compendium commissioned under Emperor Taizong of Song; frequently cited by Li Shizhen as a source.

results.translator_notes: The phrase 淋露骨立 is a classical idiom meaning extreme emaciation, literally 'drenched and exposed, standing as bare bones.' The dosage unit 兩 (liǎng) is a traditional Chinese weight measure, approximately 37.3 g in the Ming dynasty context, though practical apothecary liang could vary. The instruction 如茶點服 literally means 'serve/administer as one would prepare powdered tea,' referencing the Song–Ming practice of whisking powdered tea in hot water rather than brewing loose leaves.
